topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST502 - SHIP STRUCTURES I
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Shipbuilding Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Shipbuilding materials** are defined as the carefully selected engineering materials — primarily structural steels, non-ferrous alloys, and advanced composites — that must satisfy classification society rules (IRS, Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, BV) and international standards (ISO, ASTM, EN) to ensure the structural integrity, seaworthiness, fatigue resistance, and corrosion durability of a marine vessel throughout its operational service life (typically **25–30 years**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCNST502, Module 1):**
> The syllabus explicitly requires students to understand the **classification, mechanical properties, and selection criteria** of ferrous and non-ferrous materials used in hull construction, superstructure, and outfitting components. Material selection directly governs plate thickness, frame spacing, and scantling calculations covered later in Modules 2–4.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Marine Material Specification

A shipbuilding material must simultaneously satisfy **three competing engineering demands**:

| Pillar | Demand | Practical Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| **Strength** | Yield & tensile strength | Determines plate thickness and section modulus |
| **Ductility** | Elongation % and impact toughness | Resists brittle fracture in cold seas |
| **Weldability** | Carbon equivalent (CE) | Enables efficient fabrication in the yard |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Marine Material Trinity"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Cooking Pot Metaphor**
> Think of a ship's hull as a giant cooking pot that must:
> 1. **Hold shape under weight** (the ship's own mass + cargo) → requires **strength** (a thick, sturdy pot).
> 2. **Not shatter if dropped on a cold stone floor** (wave impact in icy seas) → requires **toughness/ductility** (a pot that bends rather than snaps).
> 3. **Be joined quickly from many flat sheets** → requires **weldability** (steel that fuses easily without cracking).
> A bad pot is brittle; a bad ship is brittle. We choose **mild steel, high-tensile steel, or aluminum alloys** as the "ingredients" that balance these three needs.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Ship Structures I

The bending moment, shear force, and hull girder stress calculations (Modules 2–3) **all depend on the material's yield stress (σ_y)** appearing in the section modulus formula:

$$\sigma = \frac{M}{Z} \leq \frac{\sigma_y}{\gamma_m}$$

where **γ_m = 1.0 to 1.15** is the partial safety factor prescribed by IACS Common Structural Rules. A wrong material assumption (e.g., using mild steel where HT steel is required) leads to a **direct under-design of the hull girder** — a catastrophic error.

## 1.5 Broad Classification of Shipbuilding Materials

Shipbuilding materials are conventionally grouped into three families:

1. **Ferrous Metals (≈ 90% of hull by weight)** — Mild steel, High-Tensile (HT) steel, Austenitic stainless steel.
2. **Non-Ferrous Metals (≈ 8%)** — Aluminum alloys (superstructures), Copper-nickel (piping), Bronze (propellers, valves), Titanium (submarines).
3. **Non-Metallic Materials (≈ 2%)** — FRP composites, GRP, marine plywood, GRP sandwich panels, rubber, cork, paints, and insulation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mild Steel (MS) — The Backbone of Shipbuilding

**Mild steel** is a low-carbon steel (C ≤ **0.23%**) historically forming **80–85%** of a merchant vessel's hull.

### 2.1.1 Chemical Composition (Typical Grade A per ASTM A131 / IRS Rules)

| Element | Symbol | Typical % |
|---|---|---|
| Carbon | C | 0.21 – 0.23 |
| Manganese | Mn | 0.60 – 1.20 |
| Silicon | Si | 0.10 – 0.35 |
| Sulphur | S | ≤ 0.040 |
| Phosphorus | P | ≤ 0.040 |

### 2.1.2 Mechanical Properties (Grade A — As Rolled)

- **Yield Stress (σ_y):** **235 N/mm² = 235 MPa**
- **Ultimate Tensile Stress (σ_u):** **400 – 520 N/mm²**
- **Elongation at fracture (A):** **≥ 22%** (on 50 mm gauge length)
- **Charpy V-Notch Impact Energy:** **27 J at 0°C** (transverse)
- **Density (ρ):** **7850 kg/m³**
- **Young's Modulus (E):** **2.05 × 10⁵ N/mm² = 205 GPa**
- **Poisson's ratio (ν):** **0.30**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mild Steel Dominates Hulls:**
> 1. Excellent weldability (low CE).
> 2. Predictable ductile failure mode.
> 3. Cheap and globally available.
> 4. Easily formed, rolled, and flame-cut.

## 2.2 High-Tensile (HT) Steel — The Lightweight Champion

**High-Tensile steel** is a low-alloy steel with controlled additions of Mn, Nb, V, or Ti that produce **grain refinement** and **precipitation hardening**, raising yield strength without sacrificing weldability.

### 2.2.1 Grades Recognized by IACS (Common Structural Rules)

| IACS Grade | σ_y (MPa) | σ_u (MPa) | Elongation % | Charpy @ 0°C |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **AH 32** | 315 | 440 – 590 | 22 | 31 J |
| **DH 32** | 315 | 440 – 590 | 22 | 31 J |
| **EH 32** | 315 | 440 – 590 | 22 | 31 J |
| **AH 36** | 355 | 490 – 620 | 21 | 34 J |
| **DH 36** | 355 | 490 – 620 | 21 | 34 J |
| **EH 36** | 355 | 490 – 620 | 21 | 34 J |
| **AH 40** | 390 | 510 – 660 | 20 | 39 J |
| **DH 40 / EH 40** | 390 | 510 – 660 | 20 | 39 J |

> **Reading the grade code:**
> - **A / D / E** = toughness level (A: 0°C, D: –20°C, E: –40°C).
> - **32 / 36 / 40** = yield stress in ksi (× 10) — i.e., 32 ksi ≈ 220 MPa, **rounded to 315 MPa** in SI tables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Design Insight (KTU 2024):**
> For every 1% reduction in hull weight achieved by upgrading from Grade A (235 MPa) to AH 36 (355 MPa), a vessel of **L = 150 m** saves roughly **100–150 tonnes** of steel — translating to **increased deadweight, lower fuel consumption, and higher freight earnings**.

## 2.3 Carbon Equivalent (CE) and Weldability

The **IIW (International Institute of Welding) Carbon Equivalent** governs preheat requirements and HAZ (Heat-Affected Zone) cracking:

$$CE = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \frac{Ni + Cu}{15}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Weldability Rule of Thumb (Marine Practice):**
> - **CE ≤ 0.40** → No preheat required for plates ≤ 25 mm.
> - **0.40 < CE ≤ 0.55** → Preheat to **100–150°C**.
> - **CE > 0.55** → Special low-hydrogen electrodes, post-weld heat treatment (PWHT).

For Grade A plate, CE is typically **0.30–0.35**, which is why mild steel welds cleanly.

## 2.4 Aluminum Alloys in Ships

Used primarily in **superstructures, deckhouses, helidecks, and fast-ferry hulls** to reduce topside weight (improving stability) and increase speed.

**Two dominant marine grades:**

| Alloy | Temper | σ_y (MPa) | σ_u (MPa) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **5083** | H116 / H321 | 215 – 240 | 305 – 385 | Hull plates of fast ferries, patrol boats |
| **5086** | H116 | 195 – 240 | 275 – 350 | Superstructures |
| **6082** | T6 | 260 – 310 | 310 – 340 | Masts, helideck structures |
| **6061** | T6 | 240 | 290 | Deck fittings |

- **Density (ρ_Al):** **2700 kg/m³** (≈ **34.4%** of steel).
- **E_Al:** **70 GPa** (≈ **1/3** of steel).
- **Linear Thermal Expansion:** **23 × 10⁻⁶ /°C** (≈ **twice** that of steel).

> [!WARNING]
> **Module 1 Pitfall:** Students often forget that **E_Al is only 1/3 of E_steel**, so an aluminum beam of the same section is **3× more flexible**. Deflection (not strength) usually governs aluminum scantling.

## 2.5 Other Shipbuilding Materials

- **Austenitic Stainless Steel (AISI 304 / 316L):** Used in chemical tankers, LNG membrane tanks. σ_y ≈ 220 MPa, σ_u ≈ 520 MPa, but excellent corrosion resistance.
- **Copper-Nickel (90/10 and 70/30):** Sea-water piping systems; biofouling-resistant.
- **Manganese Bronze & Nickel-Aluminum Bronze (NAB):** Propellers, valve bodies, stern tubes. NAB: σ_y ≈ 250–300 MPa.
- **Titanium Grade 2 / Grade 5:** Submarine pressure hulls, seawater cooling systems. Excellent strength-to-weight ratio, immune to chloride pitting.
- **FRP / GRP Composites:** Small craft (< 50 m), mine countermeasure vessels, patrol boats. E-glass + isophthalic polyester resin.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table — it covers ~70% of Module 1 numerical questions in KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Value / Unit | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Yield stress of MS Grade A | σ_y | **235 MPa** | Hull girder still water bending |
| 2 | Yield stress of HT AH 32/36 | σ_y | **315 / 355 MPa** | Strengthened deck & bottom |
| 3 | Young's Modulus of steel | E | **205 GPa** | Deflection, buckling checks |
| 4 | Young's Modulus of aluminum | E_Al | **70 GPa** | Superstructure deflection |
| 5 | Density of steel | ρ_s | **7850 kg/m³** | Weight estimation |
| 6 | Density of aluminum | ρ_Al | **2700 kg/m³** | Weight saving calc |
| 7 | Bending stress in hull girder | σ = M / Z ≤ σ_y / γ_m | N/mm² | Scantling design |
| 8 | Carbon Equivalent (IIW) | CE = C + Mn/6 + (Cr+Mo+V)/5 + (Ni+Cu)/15 | dimensionless | Weld preheat decision |
| 9 | Charpy impact (Grade A) | KV | **27 J @ 0°C** | Toughness acceptance |
| 10 | Weight saving ratio | (ρ_s – ρ_Al)/ρ_s = 0.656 | 65.6% | Aluminum vs. steel mass |
| 11 | Section modulus scaling | Z_req ∝ 1/σ_y | mm³ | Plate thickness reduction |
| 12 | Linear thermal expansion (steel) | α | **12 × 10⁻⁶ /°C** | Thermal stress |
| 13 | Linear thermal expansion (Al) | α_Al | **23 × 10⁻⁶ /°C** | Thermal stress |

## 2.7 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

- **Classification societies** (IRS, Lloyd's, DNV) publish "Rule Books" that quote **minimum σ_y values** for every structural member. The student of KTU must know which grade to specify when designing.
- **Welding defects** (e.g., hydrogen-induced cracking) are statistically the **#1 cause of structural failures** in new ships — directly traceable to wrong material grade or missing preheat.
- **Weight optimization** is the #1 driver in commercial ship design — hence the 60% market share of HT steel in modern bulk carriers and container ships.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why Upgrading to HT 36 Reduces Plate Thickness

### Problem Statement
A flat plate panel in the deck of a container ship is subjected to a bending moment **M = 1200 kN·m per metre width**. Compute the required plate thickness when (a) using Grade A mild steel (σ_y = 235 MPa), and (b) using HT Grade AH 36 (σ_y = 355 MPa). Assume a rectangular section with **b = 1000 mm** and apply partial safety factor **γ_m = 1.0**.

### Given
- M = 1200 kN·m/m = 1.2 × 10⁶ N·mm / 1000 mm
- Per-metre moment: **M_unit = 1.2 × 10⁶ N·mm / 1000 mm width**
- σ_yA = 235 N/mm²
- σ_yAH36 = 355 N/mm²
- γ_m = 1.0
- b = 1000 mm

### Step 1 — Section Modulus of a Rectangular Plate Strip

For a rectangular cross-section (b × t) per unit width, the elastic section modulus is:

$$Z = \frac{b \cdot t^{2}}{6}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Allowable Bending Stress Equation

$$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{M}{Z} \leq \frac{\sigma_y}{\gamma_m}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for Plate Thickness t

$$\frac{M}{Z} \leq \frac{\sigma_y}{\gamma_m} \implies \frac{6 M}{b \cdot t^{2}} \leq \frac{\sigma_y}{\gamma_m}$$

$$t^{2} \geq \frac{6 M \cdot \gamma_m}{b \cdot \sigma_y} \implies t \geq \sqrt{\frac{6 M \cdot \gamma_m}{b \cdot \sigma_y}}$$

### Step 4 — Case (a): Mild Steel Grade A (σ_y = 235 MPa)

For a 1-metre wide strip, M = 1.2 × 10⁶ N·mm:

$$t_A \geq \sqrt{\frac{6 \times 1.2 \times 10^{6} \times 1.0}{1000 \times 235}}$$

$$t_A \geq \sqrt{\frac{7.2 \times 10^{6}}{2.35 \times 10^{5}}}$$

$$t_A \geq \sqrt{30.638} \approx 5.535 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 5 — Case (b): High Tensile AH 36 (σ_y = 355 MPa)

$$t_{AH36} \geq \sqrt{\frac{6 \times 1.2 \times 10^{6} \times 1.0}{1000 \times 355}}$$

$$t_{AH36} \geq \sqrt{\frac{7.2 \times 10^{6}}{3.55 \times 10^{5}}}$$

$$t_{AH36} \geq \sqrt{20.282} \approx 4.503 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 6 — Thickness Reduction and Mass Saving

$$\Delta t = 5.535 - 4.503 = 1.032 \text{ mm}$$

Mass saving per m²:

$$\Delta m = \rho_s \cdot \Delta t = 7850 \times 1.032 \times 10^{-3} = 8.10 \text{ kg/m}^{2}$$

Percentage reduction:

$$\% \text{ saving} = \frac{\Delta t}{t_A} \times 100 = \frac{1.032}{5.535} \times 100 \approx 18.6\%$$

### Step 7 — Engineering Interpretation

By upgrading from Grade A to AH 36, **plate thickness drops by ~18.6%**, saving **~8 kg per m²**. For a 200 m × 30 m deck area (6000 m²), the steel saving is:

$$m_{saved} = 8.10 \times 6000 = 48{,}600 \text{ kg} = 48.6 \text{ tonnes}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world payoff:** A 48.6-tonne reduction in steel mass allows either a 48.6-tonne increase in deadweight (more cargo → more revenue) **or** a 48.6-tonne reduction in fuel consumption over the ship's life.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Carbon Equivalent and Preheat Temperature

### Problem Statement
A shipyard is welding a 30 mm thick plate with the following ladle analysis: **C = 0.18%, Mn = 1.30%, Cr = 0.10%, Mo = 0.05%, V = 0.02%, Ni = 0.05%, Cu = 0.10%**. Compute the IIW Carbon Equivalent and recommend the preheat temperature.

### Step 1 — Plug into IIW Formula

$$CE = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \frac{Ni + Cu}{15}$$

$$CE = 0.18 + \frac{1.30}{6} + \frac{0.10 + 0.05 + 0.02}{5} + \frac{0.05 + 0.10}{15}$$

### Step 2 — Evaluate Each Term

$$CE = 0.18 + 0.2167 + \frac{0.17}{5} + \frac{0.15}{15}$$

$$CE = 0.18 + 0.2167 + 0.0340 + 0.0100$$

$$CE = 0.4407 \approx 0.44$$

### Step 3 — Preheat Recommendation (per AWS D1.1 / IRS Rules)

For t = 30 mm and CE = 0.44, the steel falls in the band **0.40 < CE ≤ 0.55**, therefore:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recommended Preheat: 100°C minimum**, with interpass temperature maintained at **150°C max**. Use **low-hydrogen electrodes (e.g., E7018, basic-coated)** baked at 350°C for 1 hour before use.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Material Selection in a Real Ship

> [!NOTE]
> This table is a **favourite KTU viva / short-answer question**. Memorize the logic.

| Ship Component | Material Chosen | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Hull bottom plates (tankers) | HT Grade DH 36 (355 MPa) | High tensile stress; thick plate; saves mass |
| Hull side shell (amidships) | HT Grade AH 32 (315 MPa) | Moderate stress; economic balance |
| Stem and stern frame | Cast steel or forged steel | High impact loading, complex shape |
| Superstructure | Aluminum 5083 / 5086 | Weight saving, stability, low gravity center |
| Propeller | NAB (Nickel-Aluminum Bronze) | Cavitation resistance, corrosion fatigue |
| Sea-water piping | Cu-Ni 90/10 | Biofouling resistance, low corrosion |
| LNG membrane tank | Austenitic SS 304L | Cryogenic toughness at –163°C |
| Submarine pressure hull | Titanium Grade 5 (Ti-6Al-4V) | Strength-to-weight, non-magnetic |
| Lifeboats, fast ferries | FRP / GRP | Lightweight, no corrosion, mass-producible |
| Masts and helideck | Aluminum 6082-T6 | High strength, low weight |
| Rudder, shaft bracket | Forged steel + cast steel | Heavy concentrated load |
| Boiler, economizer | Carbon steel SA-516 | High-temperature creep resistance |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Material Classification Flow Chart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SHIPBUILDING MATERIALS] --> B[FERROUS METALS - 90 percent]
    A --> C[NON-FERROUS METALS - 8 percent]
    A --> D[NON-METALLIC MATERIALS - 2 percent]

    B --> B1[Mild Steel Grade A - 235 MPa]
    B --> B2[High Tensile HT 32 - 315 MPa]
    B --> B3[High Tensile HT 36 - 355 MPa]
    B --> B4[High Tensile HT 40 - 390 MPa]
    B --> B5[Austenitic Stainless 304 316L]

    C --> C1[Aluminum Alloys 5083 5086 6082]
    C --> C2[Copper Nickel 90 10 and 70 30]
    C --> C3[Nickel Aluminum Bronze NAB]
    C --> C4[Titanium Grade 2 and Grade 5]

    D --> D1[FRP GRP Composites]
    D --> D2[Marine Plywood]
    D --> D3[GRP Sandwich Panels]
    D --> D4[Rubber Cork Insulation]
    D --> D5[Marine Paints Coatings]

    B1 --> E[Hull Plating - Light duty]
    B2 --> E2[Side Shell - Deck Plating]
    B3 --> E3[Bottom Plates - Strengthened Members]
    B4 --> E4[Bulk Carriers - Very High Stress]

    C1 --> F1[Superstructure - Helideck]
    C2 --> F2[Sea Water Piping]
    C3 --> F3[Propellers - Valve Bodies]
    C4 --> F4[Submarine Hulls]
```

## 4.2 Material Selection Logic — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Design Requirements]
        I1[Yield Strength MPa]
        I2[Ductility Percent]
        I3[Weldability CE]
        I4[Corrosion Resistance]
        I5[Density kg per m3]
        I6[Cost per Tonne]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS[Selection Engine]
        P1{Calculate Section Modulus Zreq}
        P2{Match IACS Grade?}
        P3{Compute Carbon Equivalent}
        P4{Operating Environment?}
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Recommended Material]
        O1[Grade A MS - 235 MPa]
        O2[AH 32 HT - 315 MPa]
        O3[AH 36 HT - 355 MPa]
        O4[Aluminum 5083 H116]
        O5[Titanium Grade 5]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P3
    I4 --> P4
    I5 --> P1
    I6 --> P4

    P1 --> P2
    P3 --> P2
    P4 --> P2

    P2 --> O1
    P2 --> O2
    P2 --> O3
    P2 --> O4
    P2 --> O5
```

## 4.3 Hull Girder Stress Distribution — Why We Use Different Grades

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Hull Girder Bending Moment] --> L2{Region of Vessel}
    L2 --> R1[Amidships - HIGH M_sigma]
    L2 --> R2[Ends - LOW M_sigma]

    R1 --> S1[Bottom Plate - HT 36]
    R1 --> S2[Deck Plate - HT 36]
    R1 --> S3[Side Shell - HT 32]

    R2 --> S4[Bottom Plate - HT 32]
    R2 --> S5[Deck Plate - Grade A]
    R2 --> S6[Side Shell - Grade A]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Bending moment is **maximum at midship** and decreases toward the ends. Therefore, **expensive HT 36 is used only in the high-stress midship region** (typically 0.4 L amidships), while cheaper Grade A is used at the ends. This is a textbook KTU 2024 Module 1–2 cross-link.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**List any three mechanical properties of mild steel used in shipbuilding. State typical values.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three key mechanical properties of **mild steel (Grade A)** as per ASTM A131 / IRS Rules are:

1. **Yield Stress (σ_y):** **235 N/mm² (MPa)** — defines the onset of plastic deformation; used directly in hull girder bending calculations.

2. **Ultimate Tensile Strength (σ_u):** **400 – 520 N/mm²** — represents the maximum stress before fracture; provides a safety margin above yield.

3. **Elongation at fracture (A₅₀):** **≥ 22%** — a measure of ductility; ensures the steel deforms plastically before failing (no brittle fracture), which is critical in cold seas.

> (Additional property if asked: Charpy V-notch impact = **27 J at 0°C**.)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]**
**Define Carbon Equivalent. Why is it important in shipbuilding?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Carbon Equivalent (CE)** is a numerical index that expresses the combined weldability effect of all alloying elements (C, Mn, Cr, Mo, V, Ni, Cu) in terms of equivalent carbon percentage. The **IIW formula** is:

$$CE = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \frac{Ni + Cu}{15}$$

**Importance in shipbuilding:**
- CE predicts the **cold-cracking susceptibility** of the heat-affected zone (HAZ) during welding.
- For **CE > 0.40**, preheat (**100–150°C**) and low-hydrogen electrodes become mandatory to avoid hydrogen-induced cracking.
- Ship hulls are **mass-produced by welding**, so a wrong CE assessment can cause catastrophic weld failures in service.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Each module carries a **14-mark question with internal choice**. You must attempt either Q(A) or Q(B). Each has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Material Properties & Selection** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]

#### (a) **Compare Mild Steel and High Tensile Steel in terms of mechanical properties, weldability, and applications.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

| Property | Mild Steel (Grade A) | High Tensile Steel (AH 36) |
|---|---|---|
| **Yield Stress (σ_y)** | 235 MPa | 355 MPa |
| **Tensile Stress (σ_u)** | 400 – 520 MPa | 490 – 620 MPa |
| **Elongation %** | ≥ 22% | ≥ 21% |
| **Charpy Impact** | 27 J @ 0°C | 34 J @ 0°C |
| **Carbon Equivalent** | 0.30 – 0.35 | 0.38 – 0.45 |
| **Density** | 7850 kg/m³ | 7850 kg/m³ |
| **Cost per tonne** | Lower (~₹55,000) | Higher (~₹62,000) |
| **Weldability** | Excellent, no preheat ≤ 25 mm | Preheat required (100–150°C) for thick plates |
| **Primary Application** | Light hull plates, ends of vessel | Bottom, deck, side shell amidships |
| **Advantage** | Cheap, easy to weld | Mass saving of 18–22% |

> **Engineering verdict:** HT steel is preferred where **strength governs** (midship bottom, sheer strake), MS where **economy and weldability dominate** (end regions, non-critical stiffeners).

#### (b) **A ship hull bottom plate is subjected to a bending moment of 1500 kN·m per metre width. Calculate the required thickness using (i) Grade A mild steel, (ii) HT Grade AH 36. Take γ_m = 1.10 and partial safety factor on material as 1.0.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Given data**
- M = 1500 kN·m/m = **1.5 × 10⁶ N·mm / 1000 mm**
- σ_yA = 235 N/mm²
- σ_yAH36 = 355 N/mm²
- γ_m = 1.10 (Note: typical notation — here we treat as the partial safety factor in σ_allow)
- b = 1000 mm

**Step 2 — Section modulus required**

$$Z_{req} = \frac{M \cdot \gamma_m}{\sigma_y}$$

**Case (i) Grade A:**

$$Z_{req,A} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{6} \times 1.10}{235} = 7021.28 \text{ mm}^{3}/\text{mm}$$

**Case (ii) AH 36:**

$$Z_{req,AH36} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{6} \times 1.10}{355} = 4647.89 \text{ mm}^{3}/\text{mm}$$

**Step 3 — Thickness from Z = b·t²/6 (per mm width)**

$$t = \sqrt{\frac{6 Z_{req}}{b}}$$

**Case (i):**
$$t_A = \sqrt{\frac{6 \times 7021.28}{1}} = \sqrt{42127.7} = 205.25 \text{ mm}$$

**Case (ii):**
$$t_{AH36} = \sqrt{\frac{6 \times 4647.89}{1}} = \sqrt{27887.3} = 167.00 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Mass saving per m²**

$$m_A = 7850 \times 0.20525 = 1611.2 \text{ kg/m}^{2}$$
$$m_{AH36} = 7850 \times 0.167 = 1311.0 \text{ kg/m}^{2}$$
$$\Delta m = 300.2 \text{ kg/m}^{2} \quad (18.6\% \text{ saving})$$

> **Note:** The 205 mm figure illustrates extreme cases; in practice, plate thickness is also checked against **local bending, shear, and buckling** (Modules 2–3), which may further govern. The example is a **first-pass strength estimate** only.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Welding & Non-Ferrous Materials** [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]

#### (a) **Explain the Carbon Equivalent formula. A plate has the following chemistry: C = 0.20%, Mn = 1.40%, Cr = 0.30%, Mo = 0.10%, V = 0.04%, Ni = 0.20%, Cu = 0.15%. Compute CE and recommend preheat for a 28 mm thick plate.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Explain CE (3 marks)**
Carbon Equivalent is a numerical index expressing the effect of alloying elements on weldability. Higher CE → greater risk of hydrogen cracking. The IIW formula is:

$$CE = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \frac{Ni + Cu}{15}$$

**Step 2 — Compute each term**

- C = 0.20
- Mn/6 = 1.40/6 = 0.2333
- (Cr + Mo + V)/5 = (0.30 + 0.10 + 0.04)/5 = 0.44/5 = 0.0880
- (Ni + Cu)/15 = (0.20 + 0.15)/15 = 0.35/15 = 0.0233

**Step 3 — Sum**

$$CE = 0.20 + 0.2333 + 0.0880 + 0.0233 = 0.5446 \approx 0.54$$

**Step 4 — Preheat recommendation (per AWS D1.1 / IRS Rules)**

For t = 28 mm and CE = 0.54:
- CE falls in the band **0.40 < CE ≤ 0.60**.
- **Preheat required: 100–150°C**.
- **Interpass max: 230°C**.
- **Use low-hydrogen electrodes** (E7018 / equivalent) baked at 350°C for 1 hour minimum.
- **Post-weld inspection**: Magnetic particle testing of butt welds recommended.

#### (b) **Discuss the use of aluminum alloys and FRP composites in modern shipbuilding. Compare their advantages over mild steel.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Aluminum Alloys (4 marks)**

| Aspect | Aluminum 5083-H116 | Mild Steel Grade A |
|---|---|---|
| **Density** | **2700 kg/m³** | 7850 kg/m³ |
| **Yield Stress** | 215–240 MPa | 235 MPa |
| **E (Young's Modulus)** | **70 GPa** | 205 GPa |
| **Weight Saving** | ~65% lighter for same volume | Baseline |
| **Corrosion Resistance** | Excellent in marine atmosphere | Requires protective coating |
| **Welding Method** | MIG (GMAW), TIG (GTAW) | SMAW, FCAW, GMAW |
| **Application** | Superstructures, fast-ferry hulls, helidecks | Hull plating |

**Disadvantages:** Higher initial cost (~3× steel), lower stiffness, galvanic corrosion when joined to steel (need bi-metallic inserts), higher thermal expansion (2× steel).

**FRP Composites (3 marks)**
- Glass-Reinforced Plastic (GRP) consists of E-glass fibres embedded in polyester or vinylester resin.
- Used in **small craft < 50 m**, mine-hunters, patrol boats, lifeboats.
- **Density: 1500–1900 kg/m³**; **σ_u: 200–400 MPa**; corrosion-free; no painting; mouldable into complex shapes.
- **Disadvantages:** Low stiffness (E ≈ 10–25 GPa), fire/smoke/toxicity issues, difficult to repair, high initial tooling cost, susceptible to UV degradation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU 2024 Students Most Commonly Lose Marks on Material Questions:**

1. **Forgetting the partial safety factor γ_m.** Many students write σ = M/Z ≤ σ_y without including γ_m. KTU examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for this.

2. **Confusing σ_y values across grades.** Grade A = 235 MPa (not 250 MPa), AH 32 = 315 MPa, AH 36 = 355 MPa. The grade number is in **ksi, not MPa** — a classic trap.

3. **Using E_Al = 205 GPa** in deflection problems. Aluminum's E is **70 GPa**, exactly **1/3 of steel**. Wrong E → completely wrong deflection → 0 marks.

4. **Omitting the "Ductility ≥ 22%" point** when listing mild steel properties. Examiners expect **3–4 properties** with units and typical values.

5. **In Carbon Equivalent questions, students often add the wrong terms** (e.g., adding Mn to the third bracket instead of dividing by 6). Memorize the formula accurately.

6. **Saying "aluminum is corrosion-free"** — incorrect. Aluminum forms a protective oxide film, but it **does corrode in alkaline or low-oxygen environments**. Use precise language.

7. **Ignoring weldability.** If asked "why mild steel is preferred", always mention **low CE (≤ 0.40) → no preheat → fast fabrication**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Bulleted Summary — Revise Before KTU Exam Day.**

- ☐ **Mild Steel (MS) Grade A** is the default ship hull material: **σ_y = 235 MPa, σ_u = 400–520 MPa, A ≥ 22%, CE ≈ 0.30–0.35**.
- ☐ **High Tensile (HT) Steel grades** AH 32, AH 36, AH 40 have σ_y = **315, 355, 390 MPa** respectively.
- ☐ **A / D / E in grade name = toughness at 0°C / –20°C / –40°C** (A = normal, D = cold, E = arctic).
- ☐ **Grade number (32, 36, 40) is in ksi** (32 ksi ≈ 220 MPa, rounded to 315 in SI).
- ☐ **HT steel saves 18–22% mass** at the same strength level; used in **midship 0.4 L** region.
- ☐ **E (Young's Modulus):** Steel = **205 GPa**; Aluminum = **70 GPa** (≈ 1/3).
- ☐ **Density:** Steel = **7850 kg/m³**; Aluminum = **2700 kg/m³** (≈ 1/3); GRP ≈ **1800 kg/m³**.
- ☐ **Carbon Equivalent (IIW):** CE = C + Mn/6 + (Cr+Mo+V)/5 + (Ni+Cu)/15. **CE > 0.40 → preheat mandatory**.
- ☐ **Charpy V-notch impact for Grade A = 27 J at 0°C** (transverse specimen).
- ☐ **Aluminum alloys 5083, 5086, 6082** are the three principal marine grades.
- ☐ **NAB (Nickel-Aluminum Bronze)** is used for **propellers and stern gear** due to cavitation resistance.
- ☐ **Cu-Ni 90/10** is used for **sea-water piping** due to biofouling resistance.
- ☐ **Titanium Grade 5 (Ti-6Al-4V)** is used for **submarine pressure hulls** and **LNG membrane tanks**.
- ☐ **Austenitic stainless steel 304L/316L** for **chemical tankers and cryogenic service** (–163°C LNG).
- ☐ **Hull girder stress equation:** σ = M/Z ≤ σ_y/γ_m. Partial safety factor γ_m ≈ 1.0 to 1.15.
- ☐ **Plate thickness formula:** t = √(6 M γ_m / (b σ_y)).
- ☐ **IACS (International Association of Classification Societies)** issues **Common Structural Rules (CSR)** — these define material grades, weldability limits, and scantling requirements.
- ☐ **FRP/GRP** is preferred for small craft, mine-hunters, patrol boats; non-corrosive, lightweight, mouldable.
- ☐ **Cost ranking (approx):** MS Grade A < HT AH 32 < HT AH 36 < Aluminum 5083 < NAB < Titanium.
- ☐ **Weight optimization** is the #1 driver for HT steel in modern bulk carriers, tankers, and container ships.
- ☐ **The three competing demands** in material selection: Strength × Ductility × Weldability.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wood
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST502 - SHIP STRUCTURES I
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wood

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 1 — WOOD IN SHIP STRUCTURES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Wood** (also termed *timber* when converted into usable structural form) is a naturally occurring **anisotropic, hygroscopic, cellular composite material** produced by trees as their principal structural and conductive tissue. From a naval architecture and shipbuilding standpoint, **wood is the oldest and historically most important shipbuilding material**, used for hull planking, decking, framing, masts, keels, and interior joinery due to its high strength-to-weight ratio, natural buoyancy, ease of fabrication, and renewability.

In engineering terms, wood is a **biopolymer composite** composed primarily of **cellulose** (~40–50%), **hemicellulose** (~20–30%), and **lignin** (~20–30%), with extractives, resins, and variable moisture content completing its composition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Wood is an organic, hygroscopic structural material of biological origin, anisotropic in nature, used in ship construction where its cellular structure, moisture content, and grain orientation directly govern its strength, dimensional stability, and durability.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of wood as **nature's own reinforced concrete**:
- **Cellulose fibers** = the "steel rebar" (provide tensile strength)
- **Lignin matrix** = the "concrete" (binds fibers, provides compressive stiffness)
- **Hollow cellular structure** = makes it lightweight, like aerated concrete
- **Grain direction** = like rebar aligned in one direction (anisotropic)

Just as a steel-rebar concrete beam is strongest along the rebar, a wooden plank is strongest **along the grain** and weakest **across the grain** — this is why shipwrights carefully orient planks when building a hull.

> [!NOTE]
> **Buoyancy of Wood:** Most woods float in water (specific gravity < 1.0) because of their porous cellular structure filled with air. This is why wooden ships are inherently buoyant and historically more survivable in damage scenarios than early metal ships.

### 1.3 Classification of Timber (KTU High-Yield)

Timber is classified along multiple axes:

| Classification Basis | Categories | Examples in Shipbuilding |
|---|---|---|
| **By Botanical Group** | Softwoods (Gymnosperms/Conifers) | Teak, Deodar, Pine, Fir, Cedar |
| | Hardwoods (Angiosperms/Deciduous) | Sal, Oak, Mahogany, Sheesham, Teak* |
| **By Conversion** | Log, Baulk, Plank, Batten, Scantling | Hull planks, keel, frames |
| **By Seasoning State** | Green timber, Seasoned timber | Green for bending, seasoned for hull |
| **By Durability** | Class I (most durable) → Class V (perishable) | Teak = Class I |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Teak (Tectona grandis)** is the **gold standard** of shipbuilding timber — naturally oily, highly water-resistant, dimensionally stable, and resistant to marine borers. It is classified under hardwoods botanically but behaves with softwood-like workability.

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants for Wood

- **Density of seasoned wood:** **400 to 1000 kg/m³** (avg ≈ 650 kg/m³)
- **Specific gravity (oven-dry):** **0.30 to 0.90** (most species 0.40–0.75)
- **Fiber Saturation Point (FSP):** **25% to 30%** moisture content
- **Coefficient of thermal expansion:** Negligible (≈ 0)
- **Modulus of Elasticity (along grain):** **7 to 12 GPa** for most structural timbers
- **Ultimate Tensile Strength (along grain):** **50 to 150 MPa**
- **Ultimate Compressive Strength (along grain):** **30 to 60 MPa**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Macro-Structure of a Tree (Cross-Section View)

A cross-section of a tree trunk reveals concentric zones, each with distinct engineering properties:

1. **Pith (Medulla):** Central core, soft, weakest zone, often decayed — *never used structurally*.
2. **Heartwood:** Inner dead wood, dark colored, densest, most durable, highest resistance to decay — **preferred for ship hulls and structural members**.
3. **Sapwood:** Outer living wood, lighter in color, contains stored food, less durable, more permeable to preservatives.
4. **Cambium:** Thin living layer (cells that produce new wood and bark) — *removed when bark is stripped*.
5. **Bark (Phloem + Cork):** Outer protective layer, removed before timber is used.

**Medullary rays** run radially from pith to bark and influence *checking* and *shrinkage* patterns.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Heartwood > Sapwood in strength and durability. A 2023 KTU question directly tested: *"Why is heartwood preferred over sapwood for shipbuilding?"* — Answer: higher density, natural extractives (oils/tannins), decay resistance, and lower permeability.

## 2.2 Micro-Structure (Cellular Anatomy)

Wood is a **honeycomb of elongated tubular cells** oriented predominantly along the trunk axis. Three principal cell types govern mechanical behavior:

- **Tracheids (Softwoods):** ~90% of volume; provide both conduction and strength.
- **Vessels (Hardwoods):** Larger pores for water conduction; create the characteristic "ring-porous" or "diffuse-porous" patterns.
- **Fibres:** Thick-walled cells, primary load-bearing elements.

These cells are bonded by **lignin**, and the cell walls themselves are laminated layers of **cellulose microfibrils** (S1, S2, S3 layers of the secondary wall). The **S2 layer's microfibril angle** is the single most important microscopic parameter governing stiffness and shrinkage.

## 2.3 Physical Properties of Wood

| Property | Description | Engineering Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Color** | Heartwood darker than sapwood | Species identification |
| **Odor** | Distinct in fresh-cut wood (e.g., cedar, teak) | Detects decay & species |
| **Density** | Mass per unit volume; depends on moisture | Buoyancy, strength, fastening |
| **Moisture Content (MC)** | Weight of water / oven-dry weight × 100 | Strength, shrinkage, decay resistance |
| **Specific Gravity (G)** | Ratio of wood density to water density | Buoyancy, load calc |
| **Shrinkage/Swelling** | Anisotropic — tangential > radial > longitudinal | Dimensional stability of hull |
| **Thermal conductivity** | Low (~0.1–0.2 W/m·K) | Insulation of cabins |

## 2.4 Mechanical Properties of Wood (Anisotropic Behavior)

Wood is **anisotropic** — properties differ along three principal axes:
- **Longitudinal (L):** Along grain (strongest, stiffest)
- **Radial (R):** Across growth rings
- **Tangential (T):** Along growth rings (weakest perpendicular direction)

### Approximate Strength Ratios (Softwood, seasoned):

| Stress Type | Relative to Longitudinal |
|---|---|
| Tension parallel to grain | **1.0** (highest) |
| Compression parallel to grain | ~0.4–0.5 |
| Bending (flexure) | ~0.7–0.9 |
| Compression perpendicular to grain | ~0.1–0.15 |
| Shear parallel to grain | ~0.1–0.2 |
| Tension perpendicular to grain | **~0.02–0.05** (lowest) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often assume wood is "strong" uniformly. Always state the direction. A plank is *20–50× stronger* along grain than across grain in tension.

## 2.5 Seasoning of Timber

**Seasoning** is the controlled reduction of moisture content to a level equilibrium with service environment. It improves:
- Strength and stiffness
- Dimensional stability
- Resistance to decay
- Paint/glue adhesion

### Methods of Seasoning

| Type | Method | Duration | Quality |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Natural / Air Seasoning** | Stacked in open shed with stickered layers | 6 months – 2 years | Good, slow |
| **Artificial / Kiln Seasoning** | Controlled temperature, humidity, airflow in chamber | 2–7 days | Superior, fast |
| **Water Seasoning** | Submerged in flowing water to leach sap | 2–4 weeks | Reduces warping risk |
| **Chemical Seasoning** | Boiling in saline solution or salt seasoning | Hours | Limited use |
| **Solar Seasoning** | Greenhouse-type solar kilns | Days to weeks | Eco-friendly |

> [!NOTE]
> **Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC):** Wood in service reaches an EMC of ~12% in temperate climates, ~8% in air-conditioned interiors, and ~18% in marine/tropical exposure. Timber should be seasoned to **within ±2%** of service EMC.

## 2.6 Preservation of Wood

Wood preservation protects against:
- **Fungal decay** (rot)
- **Marine borers** (Teredo navalis, Limnoria)
- **Insects** (termites, powder-post beetles)

### Preservation Techniques

1. **Surface Application:** Brushing, spraying, dipping (least effective, temporary)
2. **Pressure Treatment:** Bethell, Lowry, Rueping processes (most effective, deep penetration)
3. **Diffusion Process:** Steeping in water-borne salts
4. **Hot-and-Cold Bath:** Osmotic impregnation with creosote
5. **Treated Timber for Marine Use:** CCA (Chromated Copper Arsenate) for piling; creosote for piling and structural members

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shipbuilding Note:** Traditionally, teak is naturally durable and used untreated. Other timbers used in ship hulls are typically **creosote-treated** under pressure for marine borer resistance.

## 2.7 Defects in Timber (KTU Frequently Tested)

| Defect | Description | Effect on Strength |
|---|---|---|
| **Knots** | Encased branch bases; disrupt grain | Severe reduction in tension & bending |
| **Shakes** | Longitudinal separations (cup, heart, star shakes) | Reduces shear resistance |
| **Checks** | Radial splits due to uneven drying | Minor, mostly cosmetic |
| **Twist** | Spiral distortion along length | Severe; unusable for planking |
| **Bow & Spring** | Out-of-plane curvature | Limits structural use |
| **Wane** | Missing wood on one or more corners (bark edge) | Reduces bearing area |
| **Decay** | Fungal attack (brown rot, white rot, soft rot) | Disqualifies timber |

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Moisture Content (MC) | $MC = \dfrac{W_1 - W_2}{W_2} \times 100$ | % | $W_1$ = wet weight, $W_2$ = oven-dry weight |
| 2 | Specific Gravity (G) | $G = \dfrac{w}{V \cdot \gamma_w}$ | dimensionless | $\gamma_w = 9.81$ **kN/m³** |
| 3 | Density of wood | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | **kg/m³** | Function of MC |
| 4 | Bulking factor | $BF = \dfrac{V_{green}}{V_{seasoned}}$ | — | Green wood may be 1.2–1.5× bulkier |
| 5 | Shrinkage % | $S = \dfrac{L_{green} - L_{dry}}{L_{green}} \times 100$ | % | Tangential > Radial > Longitudinal |
| 6 | Modulus of Elasticity (bending) | $E = \dfrac{P L^3}{48 \delta I}$ | **MPa** | For simply supported beam |
| 7 | Bending stress | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | **MPa** | $Z = I/y$ section modulus |
| 8 | Allowable stress | $\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{\sigma_{ult}}{FOS}$ | **MPa** | FOS = 4 to 6 typically |
| 9 | Safe load on column (Euler) | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2}$ | **N** | Slenderness ratio check required |
| 10 | Shear parallel to grain | $\tau = \dfrac{3V}{2bh}$ | **MPa** | For rectangular section |

> [!NOTE]
> In all wood design, **FOS = 4 to 6** is mandatory due to natural variability, knots, and moisture sensitivity. Marine classification societies (Lloyd's, IRS, DNV) prescribe specific allowable stresses per species and grade.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Moisture Content Calculation

**Statement:** A wooden plank of wet weight $W_1$ = **12.5 kg** is oven-dried to a constant weight $W_2$ = **10.0 kg**. Calculate its moisture content.

**Step 1: Identify the inputs**

$$W_1 = 12.5 \text{ kg (wet weight)}, \quad W_2 = 10.0 \text{ kg (oven-dry weight)}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Moisture Content formula**

$$MC = \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_2} \times 100$$

**Step 3: Substitute numerical values**

$$MC = \frac{12.5 - 10.0}{10.0} \times 100$$

**Step 4: Compute the difference in numerator**

$$MC = \frac{2.5}{10.0} \times 100$$

**Step 5: Perform division**

$$MC = 0.25 \times 100$$

$$\boxed{MC = 25\%}$$

**Interpretation:** Since MC = 25% which equals the **Fiber Saturation Point (FSP)** of wood, the timber is at the threshold below which further drying will cause shrinkage and strength gain. Above FSP, free water evaporates but no dimensional change occurs.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical:** For marine use, MC should be brought down to **12–18%** depending on whether the timber is used above waterline or in constantly wet conditions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Specific Gravity & Buoyancy of a Wooden Hull Block

**Problem:** A teak block has volume $V$ = **0.05 m³** and weighs $w$ = **32.5 kg** in air. Determine: (a) Specific gravity, (b) Buoyant force in seawater ($\gamma_{sw}$ = **10.05 kN/m³**), (c) Net downward force.

**Given:**
- $V = 0.05 \text{ m}^3$
- $m = 32.5 \text{ kg}$
- $\gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$
- $\gamma_{sw} = 10.05 \text{ kN/m}^3$

### Part (a): Specific Gravity

**Step 1: Compute the weight of the wood block**

$$W = m \cdot g = 32.5 \times 9.81$$

$$W = 318.825 \text{ N} = 0.3188 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Apply specific gravity formula**

$$G = \frac{w}{V \cdot \gamma_w}$$

$$G = \frac{0.3188}{0.05 \times 9.81}$$

**Step 3: Compute denominator**

$$G = \frac{0.3188}{0.4905}$$

$$\boxed{G = 0.65}$$

**Interpretation:** Since $G < 1$, the teak block **floats in fresh water** — consistent with teak's specific gravity of **0.55–0.70**.

### Part (b): Buoyant Force in Seawater

$$F_B = V \cdot \gamma_{sw}$$

$$F_B = 0.05 \times 10.05$$

$$\boxed{F_B = 0.5025 \text{ kN} = 502.5 \text{ N}}$$

### Part (c): Net Force (Downward)

$$F_{net} = W - F_B = 0.3188 - 0.5025$$

$$\boxed{F_{net} = -0.1837 \text{ kN (upward)}}$$

The negative sign confirms the block **floats** with a net upward force of **183.7 N** — the reserve buoyancy of this teak block.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shipbuilding Application:** The reserve buoyancy of timber components (floors, keels, side planks) is a critical factor in **damage stability** calculations. A wooden vessel can sustain significant hull breach and still remain afloat — one reason wood remained dominant in shipbuilding for millennia.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bending Stress in a Hull Deck Plank

**Problem:** A simply supported deck plank of span $L$ = **2.5 m**, rectangular cross-section $b$ = **150 mm**, $h$ = **50 mm** carries a uniformly distributed load $w$ = **4 kN/m** (including personnel, snow, equipment). Determine: (a) Maximum bending moment, (b) Section modulus, (c) Maximum bending stress, (d) Factor of safety if $\sigma_{ult}$ = **50 MPa** in bending.

### Part (a): Maximum Bending Moment (UDL on simply supported beam)

**Step 1: Apply the standard formula**

$$M_{max} = \frac{wL^2}{8}$$

**Step 2: Substitute numerical values**

$$M_{max} = \frac{4 \times (2.5)^2}{8}$$

**Step 3: Compute square and product**

$$M_{max} = \frac{4 \times 6.25}{8} = \frac{25}{8}$$

$$\boxed{M_{max} = 3.125 \text{ kN·m} = 3.125 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}}$$

### Part (b): Section Modulus (Rectangular Section)

$$Z = \frac{bh^2}{6}$$

$$Z = \frac{150 \times (50)^2}{6}$$

**Step 2: Compute the square**

$$Z = \frac{150 \times 2500}{6} = \frac{375000}{6}$$

$$\boxed{Z = 62500 \text{ mm}^3}$$

### Part (c): Maximum Bending Stress

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values (consistent units in N and mm)**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{3.125 \times 10^6}{62500}$$

$$\sigma_{max} = 50 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{max} = 50 \text{ MPa}}$$

### Part (d): Factor of Safety

$$FOS = \frac{\sigma_{ult}}{\sigma_{max}}$$

**Step 2: Substitute**

$$FOS = \frac{50}{50} = 1.0$$

$$\boxed{FOS = 1.0 \text{ (UNSAFE — material at failure)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** This deck plank design is **unsafe**. For marine structural timber, **FOS ≥ 4** is required by classification societies. Either: (i) increase $h$ to **~75 mm** (which gives $\sigma_{max}$ = 22.2 MPa, FOS ≈ 2.25 — still marginal), or (ii) use a stronger timber (teak, oak with $\sigma_{ult}$ ≈ **80–100 MPa**), or (iii) reduce the span by adding supporting beams.

### Revised Safe Design (with $h$ = **100 mm**, $b$ = **150 mm**)

$$Z_{new} = \frac{150 \times (100)^2}{6} = \frac{150 \times 10000}{6} = 250000 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$\sigma_{new} = \frac{3.125 \times 10^6}{250000} = 12.5 \text{ MPa}$$

$$FOS_{new} = \frac{50}{12.5} = 4.0 \text{ ✓ Acceptable}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Insight:** Doubling the plank depth reduces bending stress by a factor of 4. Marine architects always prefer to **increase depth** rather than width when increasing bending capacity of timber — this is the principle of geometric section efficiency.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Slenderness and Euler Buckling of a Mast

**Problem:** A wooden mast has circular cross-section, $d$ = **200 mm**, length $L$ = **8 m**, fixed at base, free at top (effective length $L_e = 2L = 16$ m). $E$ = **10 GPa**. Find the critical buckling load.

**Step 1: Effective length**

$$L_e = 2L = 2 \times 8 = 16 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Moment of inertia (circular section)**

$$I = \frac{\pi d^4}{64}$$

$$I = \frac{\pi \times (0.2)^4}{64}$$

**Step 3: Compute $d^4$**

$$I = \frac{\pi \times 0.0016}{64} = \frac{0.0050265}{64}$$

$$\boxed{I = 7.854 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^4}$$

**Step 4: Apply Euler's formula**

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2}$$

**Step 5: Substitute values**

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \times 10 \times 10^9 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-5}}{(16)^2}$$

**Step 6: Compute numerator**

$$P_{cr} = \frac{9.8696 \times 10^9 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-5}}{256}$$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{7.753 \times 10^5}{256}$$

$$\boxed{P_{cr} \approx 3028 \text{ N} \approx 3.03 \text{ kN}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Important Check:** Euler's formula is valid only for **long columns** where slenderness ratio $\lambda = L_e / r > 180$ (for timber). Here, $r = \sqrt{I/A} = d/4 = 50$ mm, so $\lambda = 16000/50 = 320 > 180$ — Euler's formula **applies**. For shorter columns, Johnson's formula or crushing stress governs.

### Analytical Matrix — Wood vs. Steel vs. Concrete (Ship Material Trade-Off)

| Property | Wood (Teak) | Mild Steel | Concrete |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density (kg/m³) | **650** | 7850 | 2400 |
| Tensile strength (MPa) | **80–100** | 410 | 3–5 |
| Compressive strength (MPa) | **40–50** | 250 | 25–40 |
| E (GPa) | **10** | 200 | 30 |
| Specific strength (strength/density) | **0.123** | 0.052 | 0.002–0.017 |
| Buoyant? | **Yes** (G<1) | No | No |
| Corrosion resistance | **High (teak)** | Poor (needs coating) | Good |
| Renewable? | **Yes** | No | Partial |
| Repair at sea? | **Easy** | Difficult | Very difficult |

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Teak's specific strength (0.123) is more than **2× that of steel** — a fundamental reason wooden sailing ships were remarkably efficient structures for centuries.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Macro-Structure of Tree Trunk (Cross-Section)

This diagram illustrates the concentric zones of a tree trunk, the most fundamental concept in timber identification and quality assessment.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["PITH (Central Core)"] --> B["HEARTWOOD (Inner Dead Wood)"]
    B --> C["SAPWOOD (Outer Living Wood)"]
    C --> D["CAMBIUM (Growth Layer)"]
    D --> E["INNER BARK (Phloem)"]
    E --> F["OUTER BARK (Cork Protection)"]
    G["MEDULLARY RAYS (Radial Transport)"] -.-> B
    G -.-> C
    H["ANNUAL GROWTH RINGS (Spring + Summer Wood)"] -.-> B
    H -.-> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** Heartwood is densest and contains natural extractives (oils, tannins, resins) that resist decay. Sapwood is nutrient-rich and decay-prone unless pressure-treated with preservatives.

## 4.2 Anisotropic Behavior of Wood

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["WOOD SAMPLE"] --> B["LONGITUDINAL (Along Grain)"]
    A --> C["RADIAL (Across Rings)"]
    A --> D["TANGENTIAL (Along Rings)"]
    B --> E["HIGHEST Strength and Stiffness"]
    C --> F["MODERATE Strength"]
    D --> G["LOWEST Strength (Most Shrinkage)"]
    H["Shrinkage Ratios: Tangential = 1.0 | Radial = 0.5 | Longitudinal = 0.01"] -.-> F
    H -.-> G
    H -.-> E
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Wood Material Flow in Ship Construction

This flow maps how raw wood becomes a ship structural member — the standard industrial process from forest to hull plank.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1["RAW TREE (Forest)"] --> P1["FELLING"]
    P1 --> P2["CROSS-CUTTING (Logs)"]
    P2 --> P3["CONVERSION (Sawmill: Plank, Baulk, Scantling)"]
    P3 --> P4["SEASONING (Air / Kiln)"]
    P4 --> Q1["QUALITY GRADING (Defect Inspection)"]
    Q1 --> Q2{"DEFECT FREE?"}
    Q2 -- NO --> Q2A["REJECT / CUT-OUT"]
    Q2 -- YES --> P5["PRESERVATION (CCA / Creosote)"]
    P5 --> P6["MECHANICAL TEST (Bending, Compression)"]
    P6 --> P7["CERTIFICATION (Lloyd's / IRS Grade)"]
    P7 --> S1["SHIPBUILDING USE"]
    S1 --> S2["HULL PLANKING"]
    S1 --> S3["DECK PLANKING"]
    S1 --> S4["FRAMING (Floors, Frames)"]
    S1 --> S5["KEEL and STEM"]
    S1 --> S6["MAST and SPAR"]
    S1 --> S7["INTERIOR JOINERY"]
```

## 4.4 Classification Decision Tree for Timber Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["TIMBER SPECIES"] --> Q1{"BOTANICAL TYPE?"}
    Q1 -- GYMNOSPERM --> SW["SOFTWOOD (Pine, Fir, Cedar, Deodar)"]
    Q1 -- ANGIOSPERM --> HW["HARDWOOD (Teak, Oak, Sal, Mahogany)"]
    SW --> Q2{"MARINE USE?"}
    HW --> Q2{"MARINE USE?"}
    Q2 -- YES --> Q3{"NATURAL DURABILITY SUFFICIENT?"}
    Q2 -- NO --> INLAND["Inland Construction (Light framing)"]
    Q3 -- YES (TEAK --> MAR1["USE AS-IS (Teak, Cedar, Sal)"]
    Q3 -- NO --> MAR2["PRESSURE TREAT (Creosote, CCA)"]
    MAR1 --> APP["APPLICATION: Hull Planking, Deck, Mast"]
    MAR2 --> APP
    INLAND --> APP2["APPLICATION: Frames, Beams, Doors"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** Teak (Tectona grandis) is unique in being both botanically a hardwood AND naturally marine-durable due to its **tectoquinones** — natural oils that repel marine borers. It is the **preferred timber for premium shipbuilding** worldwide.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q:** Define **moisture content** of wood. What is the significance of the **fiber saturation point (FSP)**?

**Model Answer:**

Moisture content (MC) of wood is defined as the ratio of the weight of water contained in the wood to the oven-dry weight of the wood, expressed as a percentage.

$$MC = \frac{W_{wet} - W_{dry}}{W_{dry}} \times 100 \, [\%]$$

The **Fiber Saturation Point (FSP)** is the moisture content at which all free water in the cell cavities has evaporated, but the cell walls are still completely saturated with bound water. For most species, FSP lies between **25% and 30%**.

**Significance of FSP:**
1. **Below FSP:** Wood begins to shrink and gain strength as water leaves the cell walls.
2. **Above FSP:** No dimensional change occurs with moisture loss; only weight decreases.
3. **FSP marks the threshold** beyond which wood becomes susceptible to decay (fungi require MC > 20% to grow).
4. All engineering design values (strength, elastic modulus) are reported at standardized MC (usually 12%).

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2023]**

**Q:** Distinguish between **heartwood** and **sapwood**. Why is heartwood preferred for shipbuilding?

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Heartwood | Sapwood |
|---|---|---|
| Location | Inner zone of trunk | Outer zone, just under bark |
| Living cells? | Dead cells | Living, physiologically active |
| Color | Darker | Lighter |
| Density | Higher | Lower |
| Natural extractives | Rich in oils, tannins, resins | Low |
| Durability | High (naturally decay-resistant) | Low (prone to decay) |
| Permeability | Low (resists preservatives) | High (absorbs preservatives well) |

**Why heartwood is preferred for shipbuilding:**
1. Higher natural **durability** due to deposited extractives.
2. Greater **density and strength**.
3. Better **dimensional stability** — less shrinkage and warping.
4. Higher **resistance to marine borers** and fungal attack.
5. **Longer service life** in marine environment without chemical treatment.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### 🔹 Question A (Choice 1) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **macro-structure of a tree trunk**. Discuss the engineering significance of each component in ship construction. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A wooden beam of rectangular cross-section **120 mm × 200 mm** and span **3 m** is simply supported and carries a uniformly distributed load of **6 kN/m** (inclusive of self-weight). If the allowable bending stress for the timber is **10 MPa**, check the adequacy of the section. Take $E$ = **10 GPa**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

### Part (a) — Macro-Structure of Tree Trunk (7 Marks)

A cross-section of a tree trunk reveals the following zones from centre to periphery:

**1. Pith (Medulla):** The central core of soft, spongy tissue. In young trees, it is the original growth point. In mature timber, it is **decomposed and weak**, with low strength and high shrinkage. **Not used structurally.**

**2. Heartwood:** The inner dead wood that no longer conducts sap. It is impregnated with **oils, resins, tannins, and gums**, giving it a darker color, higher density, and superior natural durability. **Most desirable zone for ship construction** — used for keels, frames, planking, and masts.

**3. Sapwood:** The outer living wood that actively transports sap from roots to crown. It is lighter in color, less dense, and **highly susceptible to decay and insect attack** because it contains starches and sugars. In shipbuilding, sapwood is either **discarded or pressure-treated** with preservatives.

**4. Cambium:** A thin microscopic layer of meristematic cells that produces new wood (xylem) inward and new bark (phloem) outward. **Removed during debarking** before timber conversion.

**5. Inner Bark (Phloem):** Conducts food (sugars) from leaves to the rest of the tree. **Not present in processed timber.**

**6. Outer Bark (Cork):** Dead protective layer shielding the tree from mechanical damage, fire, and moisture loss. **Removed before conversion to timber.**

**7. Medullary Rays:** Radial bands of cells running from pith to bark that transport food radially and laterally. They cause the **figure (grain pattern)** visible on quarter-sawn surfaces and influence **checking and splitting** behavior.

**Engineering Significance Summary:**

| Zone | Role in Shipbuilding |
|---|---|
| Heartwood | Primary structural material — hull, keel, mast |
| Sapwood | Conditionally used after preservative treatment |
| Pith | Always rejected — weak, decay-prone |
| Medullary rays | Affect drying behavior and checking |

> **Diagrams:** The cross-section sketch should show all zones in proper radial sequence. (5 marks for components, 2 marks for engineering significance)

---

### Part (b) — Beam Adequacy Check (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Cross-section: $b$ = **120 mm**, $h$ = **200 mm**
- Span: $L$ = **3 m** = **3000 mm**
- UDL: $w$ = **6 kN/m** = **6 N/mm**
- Allowable bending stress: $\sigma_{allow}$ = **10 MPa** = **10 N/mm²**
- $E$ = **10 GPa** = **10000 N/mm²**

**Step 1: Compute the Maximum Bending Moment (Simply supported, UDL)**

$$M_{max} = \frac{wL^2}{8}$$

$$M_{max} = \frac{6 \times (3000)^2}{8}$$

$$M_{max} = \frac{6 \times 9{,}000{,}000}{8}$$

$$M_{max} = \frac{54{,}000{,}000}{8}$$

$$\boxed{M_{max} = 6{,}750{,}000 \text{ N·mm} = 6.75 \text{ kN·m}}$$

**Step 2: Compute the Section Modulus (Rectangular section)**

$$Z = \frac{bh^2}{6}$$

$$Z = \frac{120 \times (200)^2}{6}$$

$$Z = \frac{120 \times 40000}{6}$$

$$Z = \frac{4{,}800{,}000}{6}$$

$$\boxed{Z = 800{,}000 \text{ mm}^3}$$

**Step 3: Compute the Maximum Induced Bending Stress**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z}$$

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{6{,}750{,}000}{800{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{max} = 8.4375 \text{ MPa} \approx 8.44 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 4: Check Adequacy**

$$\sigma_{max} = 8.44 \text{ MPa} < \sigma_{allow} = 10 \text{ MPa} \quad ✓$$

$$\text{Factor of Safety} = \frac{\sigma_{allow}}{\sigma_{max}} = \frac{10}{8.4375} \approx 1.185$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examination Note:** While the section is **technically safe** ($\sigma_{max} < \sigma_{allow}$), the **Factor of Safety = 1.185 is unacceptably low** for marine timber. As per classification society norms (Lloyd's Register, IRS), **FOS ≥ 4 is mandatory** for ship structural members. Therefore, the section is **NOT ADEQUATE** for shipbuilding use.

**Step 5: Redesign for FOS = 4**

For FOS = 4, allowable working stress based on $\sigma_{ult}$ (assume 50 MPa) gives the same as $\sigma_{allow} = 10$ MPa. To get a safer FOS, redesign by increasing the depth:

Required stress: $\sigma_{required} = \sigma_{allow}/4 = 2.5$ MPa is impractical here. Better to **increase $h$ to 300 mm** (depth) to gain a factor of 2.25 in stress reduction:

$$h_{new} = h_{old} \times \sqrt{4} = 200 \times 2 = 400 \text{ mm}$$

$$Z_{new} = \frac{120 \times (400)^2}{6} = 3{,}200{,}000 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$\sigma_{new} = \frac{6{,}750{,}000}{3{,}200{,}000} = 2.11 \text{ MPa}$$

$$FOS_{new} = \frac{10}{2.11} \approx 4.74 \quad ✓ \text{ Acceptable}$$

**Step 6: Deflection Check (Deflection ≤ L/360 for ship beams)**

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{5wL^4}{384EI}$$

$$I = \frac{bh^3}{12} = \frac{120 \times (200)^3}{12} = \frac{120 \times 8{,}000{,}000}{12} = 80{,}000{,}000 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{5 \times 6 \times (3000)^4}{384 \times 10000 \times 80{,}000{,}000}$$

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{5 \times 6 \times 8.1 \times 10^{13}}{3.072 \times 10^{14}}$$

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{2.43 \times 10^{15}}{3.072 \times 10^{14}} \approx 7.91 \text{ mm}$$

$$\delta_{allow} = \frac{L}{360} = \frac{3000}{360} \approx 8.33 \text{ mm}$$

$$\delta_{max} = 7.91 \text{ mm} < 8.33 \text{ mm} \quad ✓ \text{ Deflection OK (but FOS still inadequate)}$$

**Conclusion:** Original section passes stress and deflection marginally but **FOS is too low for marine application**. **Section inadequate — redesign required with $h$ = 400 mm.**

---

### 🔹 Question B (Choice 2) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** What is **seasoning of timber**? Describe the **natural and artificial methods** of seasoning with their advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **Teak block** is to be used as a **flooring piece** on a ship's weather deck. It weighs **24 kg** wet and **18 kg** after oven-drying. Calculate: (i) Moisture content, (ii) Specific gravity if volume is **0.028 m³**, (iii) Comment on whether it is suitable for marine use. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

### Part (a) — Seasoning of Timber (7 Marks)

**Definition:** Seasoning is the **controlled process of reducing the moisture content** of freshly cut (green) timber to a level suitable for its intended use, in order to improve strength, dimensional stability, and resistance to decay.

> [!NOTE]
> Seasoning is **not merely drying** — it is the **controlled, gradual removal of moisture** to minimize drying defects (warping, checking, honeycombing) while achieving target MC.

**Classification of Seasoning Methods:**

#### I. Natural / Air Seasoning
- **Procedure:** Logs/sawn timber stacked in open sheds with **stickers** (small wooden spacers) between layers to allow air circulation.
- **Duration:** 6 months to 2 years depending on species and thickness.
- **Advantages:** Low cost, no special equipment, preserves natural color, low defect rate.
- **Limitations:** Slow, weather-dependent, MC reached is equilibrium with local climate (not below 12% in humid regions), occupies large yard space.

#### II. Artificial Seasoning
**a) Kiln Seasoning**
- **Procedure:** Timber placed in sealed chamber with controlled temperature (30–100 °C), relative humidity (30–85%), and air circulation.
- **Duration:** 2–7 days.
- **Advantages:** Fast, uniform, achieves low MC (6–8%), kills insects and fungi, year-round operation.
- **Limitations:** High capital cost, requires skilled operators, may cause surface hardening if not properly controlled.

**b) Chemical Seasoning**
- **Procedure:** Timber boiled in saline solution (NaCl) to drive out sap and pre-shrink the wood.
- **Advantages:** Reduces warping, partial sterilization.
- **Limitations:** Limited to small sections, may affect glue/paint adhesion.

**c) Water Seasoning**
- **Procedure:** Logs submerged in flowing water for 2–4 weeks to leach sap.
- **Advantages:** Reduces shrinkage, prevents end splitting, makes wood easier to work.
- **Limitations:** Wood may absorb water (counterproductive), slow, partial effect.

**d) Solar / Greenhouse Seasoning**
- **Procedure:** Timber stacked inside solar-heated greenhouse; combination of solar radiation and natural ventilation.
- **Advantages:** Low energy cost, faster than air seasoning, eco-friendly.
- **Limitations:** Limited to sunny climates, smaller capacity.

> **Comparison Table (expected in answer):**
> | Method | Duration | Final MC | Cost |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | Air | 6–24 months | 12–20% | Low |
> | Kiln | 2–7 days | 6–12% | High |
> | Water | 2–4 weeks | 25–30% (then air dry) | Low |

---

### Part (b) — Teak Block Analysis (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Wet weight: $W_1$ = **24 kg**
- Oven-dry weight: $W_2$ = **18 kg**
- Volume: $V$ = **0.028 m³**

### (i) Moisture Content

**Step 1: Apply MC formula**

$$MC = \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_2} \times 100$$

**Step 2: Substitute**

$$MC = \frac{24 - 18}{18} \times 100$$

$$MC = \frac{6}{18} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{MC = 33.33\%}$$

**Interpretation:** MC = 33.33% is **above the FSP (25–30%)**. The teak is still in "green" condition with cell cavities holding free water. Further drying is required to achieve dimensional stability.

### (ii) Specific Gravity

**Step 1: Compute the weight (mass × g)**

$$W = m \cdot g = 18 \times 9.81 = 176.58 \text{ N (oven-dry weight basis)}$$

**Step 2: Compute specific gravity**

$$G = \frac{W}{V \cdot \gamma_w}$$

$$G = \frac{176.58}{0.028 \times 9810}$$

$$G = \frac{176.58}{274.68}$$

$$\boxed{G \approx 0.643}$$

**Interpretation:** This matches **teak's typical G of 0.55–0.70**, confirming the species identification. Since $G < 1$, the teak block **floats in fresh water** — a property historically exploited in transporting teak logs by river.

### (iii) Suitability for Marine Use

**Verdict:** The block is **NOT directly suitable for marine use in its present state** for the following reasons:

1. **Moisture content too high (33.33% > FSP):** Wood above FSP is dimensionally unstable and prone to shrinkage after installation, causing gaps in the deck planking.

2. **Required MC for marine use:** 
   - **Weather deck planking:** 12–15% (to match service environment)
   - **Continuously wet (below waterline):** 18–22%

3. **Recommendation:** 
   - **Kiln-dry the teak to 14% MC** before installation.
   - Apply **marine-grade oil/preservative** (e.g., tung oil, copper naphthenate) for surface protection.
   - Use **316-grade stainless steel or silicon bronze fasteners** to avoid galvanic corrosion and staining.
   - Since teak is **naturally durable** (Class I), pressure treatment with CCA is **not required** — only the MC correction is needed.

> **Final Recommendation:** After kiln-drying to 14% MC, the teak block is **excellent material for weather deck flooring** — durable, naturally oily, dimensionally stable, and aesthetically preferred for premium vessels.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in Wood-related KTU Questions (Lose 1–2 marks per mistake):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting units conversion:** $w$ (kN/m) must be converted to N/mm consistently with $L$ in mm. Mixing units = **zero marks** for the numerical answer.
> 
> 2. **Confusing specific gravity formula direction:** Some students write $G = V \gamma_w / w$ (inverted). Always check: a known substance like teak should give G ≈ 0.6.
> 
> 3. **Omitting "oven-dry weight" in MC formula:** MC is always based on **oven-dry weight** in the denominator, not wet weight. Using wet weight gives ~half the correct value.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting FOS = 4 in marine design:** Even if stress is below allowable, KTU examiners **deduct marks** if you don't comment on FOS. Marine structures need FOS ≥ 4.
> 
> 5. **Ignoring anisotropic nature:** Don't say "wood is strong" — specify *direction*: "wood has $\sigma_{t,L} \approx 100$ MPa but $\sigma_{t,T} \approx 3$ MPa".
> 
> 6. **In seasoning questions, missing the "why":** Don't just list methods — explain the **purpose** (prevent warping, increase strength, kill fungi). Examiners reward application, not just lists.
> 
> 7. **Forgetting the effect of knots:** In a beam with knots in the tension zone, allowable bending stress is reduced by **30–50%** depending on knot size and location.

---

## 5.4 📝 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Bullet-Point High-Yield Summary:**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- **Wood** is an **anisotropic, hygroscopic, cellular biological composite** made of cellulose (40–50%) + hemicellulose (20–30%) + lignin (20–30%).
- **Heartwood** > **Sapwood** in durability and strength for ship use.
- **Specific gravity of teak** ≈ **0.55–0.70** (floats in water).
- **Fiber Saturation Point (FSP)** = **25–30%** MC — the critical threshold for shrinkage and strength gain.

### 🔑 Essential Formulas
- $MC = \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_2} \times 100$ (oven-dry basis)
- $G = \frac{w}{V \gamma_w}$ ; $\rho = m/V$
- $\sigma_{max} = M/Z = 6M/(bh^2)$
- $\delta_{max} = 5wL^4/(384EI)$
- $P_{cr} = \pi^2 E I / L_e^2$ (Euler buckling)
- $FOS \geq 4$ for marine timber design

### 🔑 Material Properties
- Density: **400–1000 kg/m³** (avg 650)
- E (along grain): **7–12 GPa**
- $\sigma_{t,L}$: **50–150 MPa** ; $\sigma_{c,L}$: **30–60 MPa** ; $\sigma_{t,T}$: **2–5 MPa** (very weak across grain)
- Specific strength (teak) > **2× that of mild steel**

### 🔑 Seasoning
- **Air seasoning** = 6 months – 2 years, cheap, weather-dependent
- **Kiln seasoning** = 2–7 days, fast, controlled, kills borers
- Target MC for marine: **12–18%** depending on service

### 🔑 Defects (Memorize the 7 main ones)
- **Knots, Shakes, Checks, Twist, Bow, Spring, Wane, Decay** — in order of importance for shipbuilding rejection.

### 🔑 Preservation
- Best method: **Pressure impregnation (Bethell/Lowry)** with creosote or CCA
- Teak requires **no preservation** due to natural oils (tectoquinones)

### 🔑 Anisotropy Ratios
- Tangential shrinkage : Radial shrinkage : Longitudinal shrinkage ≈ **1.0 : 0.5 : 0.01**
- Always orient **grain parallel to principal stress** direction.

### 🔑 Shipbuilding Applications
- **Teak** → Hull planking, decking, premium joinery
- **Oak** → Frames, keels (in traditional wooden ships)
- **Pine/Douglas Fir** → Masts, spars, deck beams
- **Sal, Deodar** → Inland ship construction, decking
- **Mahogany** → Fine joinery, interiors

### 🔑 KTU-Examiner-Favorite Numerical Topics
1. MC calculation (2-mark direct)
2. Specific gravity with unit weight method
3. Bending stress in deck beams (rectangular section)
4. Deflection check (L/360 limit)
5. Euler buckling of masts and posts

> **Best of luck for your KTU 2024 Examinations! 🚢**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steel, Properties of Steel, Stress-Strain Diagrams
Higher Tensile Steel - Properties and Application
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNST502 - SHIP STRUCTURES I > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Higher Tensile Steel - Properties and Application # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) **Higher Tensile Steel (HTS)**, in the context of ship structural engineering, refers to a category of carbon-manganese and micro-alloyed structural steels possessing a specified minimum **yield strength** substantially greater than that of ordinary mild shipbuilding steel, typically ranging from **315 N/mm²** to **390 N/mm²**, while still retaining adequate ductility, weldability, notch-toughness, and formability for marine service conditions. These steels are governed by the rules of international Classification Societies such as **Lloyd's Register (LR), American Bureau of Shipping (ABS), Bureau Veritas (BV), Det Norske Veritas (DNV), and Indian Register of Shipping (IRS)**, and conform to standards such as **IACS Unified Requirements UR W (UR S6/W1 to W27)** for hull structural steels. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** > Higher tensile steel is treated as the *advanced alternative* to mild steel for modern ship construction. Students are expected to know its grades, mechanical properties, classification society designations, and specific applications in the hull. ## 1.2 Designation System (IACS Standard) The most widely used HTS grades in shipbuilding are designated by an **alphanumeric code** as follows: | Letter Code | Meaning | Delivery Condition | |-------------|---------|-------------------| | **A** | Grade with normal strength | As-rolled / Normalized | | **D** | Grade with improved toughness | Quenched & Tempered (Q&T) | | **E** | Grade with highest toughness | Thermo-Mechanically Controlled Processed (TMCP) | The **numerical suffix** indicates the minimum yield strength in **ksi × 10** units (or, equivalently, the kg/mm² level in older notation): - **32 → 32 kgf/mm² ≈ 315 N/mm² (MPa)** - **36 → 36 kgf/mm² ≈ 355 N/mm² (MPa)** Hence, the grade **AH36** signifies: **A**-quality steel with minimum yield strength of **355 N/mm²**. > [!NOTE] > The exact yield values in SI units are: > - Grade 32 series: $\sigma_y \geq 315$ **N/mm²** (MPa) > - Grade 36 series: $\sigma_y \geq 355$ **N/mm²** (MPa) > - Mild steel (Grade A) reference: $\sigma_y \geq 235$ **N/mm²** (MPa) ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine two identical aluminium ladders being used to climb onto a ship hull. One ladder is made of a thin, cheap aluminium (like *mild steel*) — it bends easily and feels flimsy when a heavy worker steps on it. The other ladder is made of a slightly heavier but markedly stiffer special alloy (like *higher tensile steel*) — it does not bend, supports a much higher load, and is also significantly lighter than a thicker mild-steel ladder that would carry the same load. **HTS behaves exactly like this analogy for ships:** by using a steel of higher yield strength, the shipbuilder can reduce the *thickness* of plates and stiffeners, thereby **reducing the displacement (light-ship weight)**, **increasing the cargo deadweight**, and **improving the ship's stability and speed**, all while maintaining (or even increasing) the structural safety factor. ## 1.4 Why Higher Tensile Steel is Used in Modern Ships - **Weight reduction** in the hull (typically **15 % to 25 %** lighter than equivalent mild-steel structure). - **Increased payload / deadweight** without enlarging the principal dimensions. - **Improved structural performance** under combined loading (sagging, hogging, slamming). - **Reduced plate thickness** simplifies fabrication, reduces welding volume, and lowers overall cost despite higher per-tonne steel cost. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mechanical Property Requirements (Classification Society Rules) For HTS to be acceptable in primary hull structure, it must satisfy the following (typical for AH/DH/EH 32, 36 grades): | Property | Typical Specified Value | Test Standard | |----------|------------------------|---------------| | Yield Stress ($\sigma_y$) | $\geq 315$ MPa (32-grade) or $\geq 355$ MPa (36-grade) | EN 10025 / ASTM A131 | | Ultimate Tensile Stress ($\sigma_u$) | $440 - 590$ MPa (32-grade) or $490 - 620$ MPa (36-grade) | EN 10025 | | Elongation ($\% E$) | $\geq 22\%$ on $L_0 = 5.65\sqrt{S_0}$ gauge | EN 10002 | | Charpy V-Notch Impact | $\geq 34$ J at $0°$C (A-grade), $-20°$C (D-grade), $-40°$C (E-grade) | EN 10045 | | Cold Bend (180°) | No cracks on $r = 2t$ bend radius | EN ISO 7438 | ## 2.2 Chemical Composition (Typical, % by mass) | Element | Mild Steel (Grade A) | HTS Grade 32 | HTS Grade 36 | |---------|--------------------|-------------|-------------| | Carbon (C) | $\leq 0.21$ | $\leq 0.18$ | $\leq 0.18$ | | Manganese (Mn) | $0.50 - 1.20$ | $0.90 - 1.60$ | $0.90 - 1.60$ | | Silicon (Si) | $0.10 - 0.35$ | $0.10 - 0.50$ | $0.10 - 0.50$ | | Sulphur (S) | $\leq 0.035$ | $\leq 0.035$ | $\leq 0.035$ | | Phosphorus (P) | $\leq 0.035$ | $\leq 0.035$ | $\leq 0.035$ | | Micro-alloys (Nb, V, Ti) | — | Added in trace amounts | Added in trace amounts | > [!NOTE] > The **low carbon equivalent (CEV $\leq 0.45$ for 36-grade)** ensures good weldability, while micro-alloying elements refine the grain and provide precipitation strengthening. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Expression | Physical Meaning | Units | |---|--------------------|-----------------|-------| | 1 | $\sigma_y = \dfrac{F_y}{A}$ | Yield stress definition | N/mm² | | 2 | $t_{HTS} = t_{MS} \times \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_{y,MS}}{\sigma_{y,HTS}}}$ | Equivalent thickness conversion | mm | | 3 | $\Delta W = \rho \cdot A \cdot (t_{MS} - t_{HTS})$ | Weight saving per plate | kg | | 4 | $CEV = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$ | Carbon Equivalent Value (IIW) | dimensionless | | 5 | $T_{preheat} = f(CEV, t_{plate})$ | Minimum preheat for welding | °C | | 6 | $f_{s} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\rho \cdot g}$ | Specific strength index | m | | 7 | $P_{mg} = \rho \cdot g \cdot t$ | Mass per unit area of plate | kg/m² | where: - $F_y$ = yield force (N) - $A$ = cross-sectional area (mm²) - $t_{HTS}, t_{MS}$ = thickness of HTS and mild steel plate (mm) - $\sigma_{y,MS} = 235$ MPa, $\sigma_{y,HTS} = 315$ or $355$ MPa - $\rho$ = density of steel $\approx 7850$ **kg/m³** - $g$ = $9.81$ **m/s²** ## 2.4 Operational Logic of HTS Use — Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Define the design load** (bending moment, shear, pressure) acting on the plate or stiffener. 2. **Calculate the required section modulus** $Z = M / \sigma_{allow}$. 3. Since $\sigma_{allow}$ for HTS is higher (315 or 355 MPa vs 235 MPa for mild steel), the **required $Z$ decreases**. 4. For the same plate width, **$t$ may be reduced** according to the square-root relationship. 5. Reduced $t$ leads to **lower displacement** → higher payload capacity. 6. Verify **stability** (against buckling), **fatigue**, and **corrosion allowance** — these often *govern* over plain yield. 7. Confirm **weldability** via CEV and preheat temperature. > [!IMPORTANT] > The reduction in thickness is **not linear** with yield strength. Because bending stress varies linearly with distance from the neutral axis, the required section modulus scales as $1/\sigma_y$, and thickness as $1/\sqrt{\sigma_y}$. This is a *classic KTU-board favourite question*. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Equivalent Thickness of an HTS Plate **Problem statement:** A mild-steel plate of thickness $t_{MS}$ carries a bending moment $M$ per unit width. Find the equivalent thickness $t_{HTS}$ in higher tensile steel (yield $\sigma_{y,HTS}$) that produces the *same maximum bending stress* under the same moment. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Bending stress formula (per unit width, for a plate strip of width $b=1$):** $$ \sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M}{Z} $$ where $Z$ is the section modulus per unit width. **Step 2 — Section modulus of a rectangular plate strip of thickness $t$:** $$ Z = \dfrac{b \cdot t^{2}}{6} $$ For unit width, $b=1$, hence $$ Z = \dfrac{t^{2}}{6} $$ **Step 3 — Maximum bending stress in mild-steel plate:** $$ \sigma_{max,MS} = \dfrac{M \cdot 6}{t_{MS}^{2}} = \dfrac{6M}{t_{MS}^{2}} $$ **Step 4 — Maximum bending stress in HTS plate (same moment $M$):** $$ \sigma_{max,HTS} = \dfrac{6M}{t_{HTS}^{2}} $$ **Step 5 — Equate the two stresses (design requirement: same elastic strength utilisation):** $$ \dfrac{6M}{t_{MS}^{2}} = \dfrac{6M}{t_{HTS}^{2}} $$ But because the allowable stress for HTS is *higher* by the ratio $\sigma_{y,HTS}/\sigma_{y,MS}$, we instead require: $$ \dfrac{6M}{t_{HTS}^{2}} = \dfrac{6M}{t_{MS}^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_{y,MS}}{\sigma_{y,HTS}} $$ **Step 6 — Simplify and solve for $t_{HTS}$:** $$ \dfrac{1}{t_{HTS}^{2}} = \dfrac{1}{t_{MS}^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_{y,MS}}{\sigma_{y,HTS}} $$ Taking the square root of both sides: $$ t_{HTS} = t_{MS} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_{y,MS}}{\sigma_{y,HTS}}} $$ This is the **master formula** for HTS thickness selection, and is heavily tested in KTU examinations. ### Numerical Illustration Given $t_{MS} = 20$ mm, $\sigma_{y,MS} = 235$ MPa, $\sigma_{y,HTS} = 355$ MPa (AH36): $$ t_{HTS} = 20 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{235}{355}} $$ Compute the ratio: $$ \dfrac{235}{355} = 0.6620 $$ Take the square root: $$ \sqrt{0.6620} = 0.8136 $$ Hence: $$ t_{HTS} = 20 \times 0.8136 = 16.27 \text{ mm} $$ In practice, this is rounded **up** to the next standard plate thickness, typically $t_{HTS} = 16$ mm or $17$ mm depending on the yard's standard. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Weight Saving per Plate The mass per square metre of a steel plate is: $$ P_{mg} = \rho \cdot t $$ where $\rho = 7850$ **kg/m³** and $t$ is in metres. For our numerical example: $$ \Delta t = t_{MS} - t_{HTS} = 20 - 16.27 = 3.73 \text{ mm} = 0.00373 \text{ m} $$ Weight saved per square metre: $$ \Delta P_{mg} = \rho \cdot \Delta t = 7850 \times 0.00373 = 29.28 \text{ kg/m}^{2} $$ Percentage saving: $$ \%\text{ Saving} = \dfrac{\Delta t}{t_{MS}} \times 100 = \dfrac{3.73}{20} \times 100 = 18.65\% $$ > [!NOTE] > A typical HTS application yields **15 % – 25 %** weight saving in the affected structure — perfectly consistent with our calculated $18.65\%$. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Carbon Equivalent Value (CEV) — Welding Suitability The IIW (International Institute of Welding) formula is: $$ CEV = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni + Cu}{15} $$ For a typical AH36 with composition $C=0.16\%$, $Mn=1.45\%$, $Si=0.30\%$, $S=0.02\%$, $P=0.02\%$: $$ CEV = 0.16 + \dfrac{1.45}{6} + 0 + 0 $$ $$ CEV = 0.16 + 0.2417 = 0.4017 $$ This is **well below** the 0.45 limit, confirming excellent weldability. ##
