# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PCNST502]**

> Define the term **GRP**. Distinguish it from **FRP** with suitable examples of each as used in ship construction.

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

- **GRP (Glass Reinforced Plastic)** is a fibre-reinforced polymer composite in which the reinforcement phase consists of glass fibres (typically E-glass) embedded in a thermosetting polymer matrix such as polyester, vinyl ester, or epoxy resin. **[1 mark]**
- **FRP (Fibre Reinforced Plastic)** is the broader, generic class of composites reinforced with any type of fibre — glass, carbon, aramid, or boron — within a polymer matrix. **[1 mark]**
- **Distinction:** GRP is a *subset* of FRP. Example of GRP: hull of a fishing trawler made with E-glass / polyester. Example of FRP outside GRP: carbon-fibre-reinforced hull of a Visby-class corvette, or aramid-reinforced ballistic panel. **[1 mark]**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PCNST502]**

> List **four advantages** of using GRP over mild steel for ship hull construction.

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

1. **High strength-to-weight ratio** — GRP is approximately 1/5 the density of steel, giving significant weight savings (typically 50–60% for equivalent stiffness). **[1 mark]**
2. **Corrosion resistance** — GRP does not rust and is immune to most marine corrosion mechanisms; eliminates the need for painting and cathodic protection. **[0.75 mark]**
3. **Non-magnetic signature** — Critical for mine countermeasure vessels. **[0.75 mark]**
4. **Formability / Design freedom** — Complex hull shapes can be moulded in a single operation with smooth, faired surfaces; reduced fairing and outfitting work. **[0.5 mark]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, PCNST502 Module 1]**

> **(a)** With the help of neat sketches, describe the **Hand Lay-up** and **Vacuum Infusion (VARTM)** processes for manufacturing marine GRP laminates. Compare the achievable **fibre volume fraction** and **void content** of each. **[7 marks]**
>
> **(b)** A unidirectional marine GRP laminate has a fibre volume fraction $V_f = 0.35$. The fibre is E-glass with $E_f = 72$ GPa, and the matrix is isophthalic polyester with $E_m = 3.5$ GPa. Compute the **longitudinal modulus** and the **transverse modulus** of the laminate. Also compute the **density** of the composite given $\rho_f = 2560$ kg/m³ and $\rho_m = 1180$ kg/m³. **[7 marks]**

#### Part (a) Model Solution — Manufacturing Processes

**Hand Lay-up Process (3.5 marks):**

- A **female mould** of the hull (or part) is prepared with a polished surface and release agent. **[0.5 mark]**
- A **gel coat** (pigmented polyester or vinyl ester, 0.4–0.6 mm) is sprayed or brushed onto the mould — this becomes the outer cosmetic and osmotic-barrier surface. **[0.5 mark]**
- When the gel coat is tacky, a **skin coat** of chopped strand mat (CSM) is applied and wetted out with catalysed resin using brushes and rollers. **[0.5 mark]**
- Successive layers of **woven roving (WR) and CSM** are then laid up, each thoroughly wetted and consolidated with rollers to remove entrapped air. **[0.5 mark]**
- The laminate is **left to cure at room temperature** (or with mild post-cure). **[0.5 mark]**
- After cure, the laminate is **demoulded** and trimmed. **[0.5 mark]**
- **Achievable $V_f$:** typically **0.20 – 0.30**; **Void content:** **3 – 5%**. **[0.5 mark]**

**Vacuum Infusion (VARTM) Process (3.5 marks):**

- A **dry stack of reinforcement fabrics** (CSM, WR, multiaxial, core material) is laid onto the mould. **[0.5 mark]**
- A **peel ply, flow media, and vacuum bag** are placed over the stack, and the assembly is sealed at the edges with tacky tape. **[0.5 mark]**
- A **resin distribution network** (spiral tubing or flow mesh) and vacuum inlet/outlet ports are arranged. **[0.5 mark]**
- The bag is **evacuated** with a vacuum pump; atmospheric pressure (≈ 1 bar) compresses the laminate. **[0.5 mark]**
- **Catalysed resin** is drawn into the laminate through the inlet under the vacuum pressure differential. **[0.5 mark]**
- Once fully infused, the resin inlet is clamped, and the laminate is allowed to **cure under vacuum**. **[0.5 mark]**
- **Achievable $V_f$:** typically **0.45 – 0.55**; **Void content:** **< 1%**. **[0.5 mark]**

**Comparison Table (built into the answer text):**

| Parameter | Hand Lay-up | VARTM |
|---|---|---|
| $V_f$ | 0.20 – 0.30 | 0.45 – 0.55 |
| Void content | 3 – 5% | < 1% |
| Mechanical properties | Lower, variable | Higher, reproducible |
| Labour intensity | High | Moderate |
| Styrene emissions | High | Low (closed bag) |

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution — Rule of Mixtures Computation

**Given:**
- $V_f = 0.35$, hence $V_m = 1 - 0.35 = 0.65$
- $E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa
- $\rho_f = 2560$ kg/m³, $\rho_m = 1180$ kg/m³

**Step 1: Longitudinal modulus (iso-strain)** **[2 marks]**

$$E_{1L} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$$

$$E_{1L} = 72 \times 0.35 + 3.5 \times 0.65$$

$$E_{1L} = 25.2 + 2.275$$

$$\boxed{E_{1L} = 27.475 \text{ GPa} \approx 27.5 \text{ GPa}}$$

**Step 2: Transverse modulus (iso-stress)** **[2 marks]**

$$E_{2T} = \frac{E_f E_m}{V_f E_m + V_m E_f}$$

$$E_{2T} = \frac{72 \times 3.5}{0.35 \times 3.5 + 0.65 \times 72}$$

$$E_{2T} = \frac{252}{1.225 + 46.8}$$

$$E_{2T} = \frac{252}{48.025}$$

$$\boxed{E_{2T} = 5.247 \text{ GPa} \approx 5.25 \text{ GPa}}$$

**Step 3: Composite density** **[1.5 marks]**

$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$$

$$\rho_c = 2560 \times 0.35 + 1180 \times 0.65$$

$$\rho_c = 896 + 767$$

$$\boxed{\rho_c = 1663 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$

**Step 4: Comments** **[1.5 marks]**

- The longitudinal modulus is approximately **5.2 times the transverse modulus** ($27.5 / 5.25 \approx 5.2$), confirming the highly anisotropic nature of the unidirectional GRP. Real marine laminates (woven, multiaxial) are far more isotropic, with $E \approx 10 – 15$ GPa.
- The composite density of **1663 kg/m³** is roughly **21% of steel's density**, confirming the substantial lightweighting benefit.
- For shipbuilding, this unidirectional $V_f$ would not be used directly; multiaxial fabrics with similar $V_f$ but distributed orientations are preferred for balanced in-plane properties.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, PCNST502 Module 1]**

> **(a)** Explain the **Rule of Mixtures** for predicting the longitudinal and transverse Young's modulus of a unidirectional fibre-reinforced composite. State the assumptions clearly. **[7 marks]**
>
> **(b)** A shipyard is evaluating two materials for a new patrol boat hull bottom:
> - **Option 1:** Mild steel plate ($\rho_{st} = 7850$ kg/m³, $E_{st} = 210$ GPa)
> - **Option 2:** GRP sandwich laminate ($\rho_{GRP} = 1600$ kg/m³, $E_{GRP} = 12$ GPa)
>
> The bottom plating must support a uniform design pressure of $p = 30$ kN/m² over a frame spacing of $s = 0.4$ m. Determine the minimum required **thickness** of each option if the maximum bending stress is not to exceed 80 MPa (steel) and 50 MPa (GRP) respectively. Also determine the **weight per square metre** of each option and the **percentage weight saving** achieved by switching to GRP. **[7 marks]**

#### Part (a) Model Solution — Rule of Mixtures Theory

**Assumptions (3 marks):**
1. Fibres are continuous, parallel, uniformly distributed, and perfectly aligned with the loading direction. **[0.5]**
2. Perfect fibre-matrix bond; no slip at the interface. **[0.5]**
3. Both fibre and matrix behave as linear elastic materials. **[0.5]**
4. The composite is initially in a stress-free state. **[0.5]**
5. Fibre volume fraction $V_f$ and matrix volume fraction $V_m$ are well-defined and satisfy $V_f + V_m = 1$. **[0.5]**
6. No voids. **[0.5]**

**Longitudinal modulus derivation (iso-strain / Voigt model) — 2 marks:**

- When the load is applied parallel to the fibres, both phases experience the **same strain** $\varepsilon$ (iso-strain assumption).
- Force equilibrium: $\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$
- Substituting Hooke's law and cancelling $\varepsilon$: $E_c A_c = E_f A_f + E_m A_m$
- Dividing by $A_c$ and recognising area fractions as volume fractions:

$$E_{1L} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$$

**Transverse modulus derivation (iso-stress / Reuss model) — 2 marks:**

- When the load is applied perpendicular to the fibres, the same stress $\sigma$ acts in both phases (iso-stress assumption).
- The total strain is the volume-weighted sum: $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f V_f + \varepsilon_m V_m$
- Substituting Hooke's law and cancelling $\sigma$:

$$\frac{1}{E_{2T}} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}$$

$$E_{2T} = \frac{E_f E_m}{V_f E_m + V_m E_f}$$

**Discussion (built in to the 2 derivation marks):**
- The longitudinal modulus is an **upper bound** (Voigt bound) — accurate for well-bonded unidirectional composites.
- The transverse modulus is a **lower bound** (Reuss bound) — actual values are somewhat lower due to interface effects.
- For a balanced woven fabric (similar $V_f$ in 0° and 90°), the effective modulus lies between these two bounds.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution — Steel vs GRP Plating Comparison

**Step 1: Maximum bending moment per unit width** **[1 mark]**

For a simply supported strip of width $b = 1$ m under uniform load $p$:

$$M = \frac{p s^2}{8} = \frac{30 \times 10^3 \times (0.4)^2}{8} = \frac{30{,}000 \times 0.16}{8} = \frac{4800}{8}$$

$$M = 600 \text{ N·m/m}$$

**Step 2: Thickness of steel plating** **[1.5 marks]**

Section modulus per metre: $Z = b t^2 / 6 = t^2 / 6$

Maximum stress: $\sigma = M / Z = 6M / t^2 \le 80$ MPa

$$t_{steel}^2 \ge \frac{6 \times 600}{1 \times 80 \times 10^6} = \frac{3600}{80 \times 10^6} = 4.5 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$t_{steel} \ge \sqrt{4.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 6.71 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{t_{steel} = 6.71 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 3: Thickness of GRP plating** **[1.5 marks]**

$$t_{GRP}^2 \ge \frac{6 \times 600}{1 \times 50 \times 10^6} = \frac{3600}{50 \times 10^6} = 7.2 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$t_{GRP} \ge \sqrt{7.2 \times 10^{-5}} = 8.485 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{t_{GRP} = 8.49 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 4: Weight per square metre** **[2 marks]**

$$w_{steel} = \rho_{st} \times t_{steel} = 7850 \times 6.71 \times 10^{-3} = 52.67 \text{ kg/m}^2$$

$$w_{GRP} = \rho_{GRP} \times t_{GRP} = 1600 \times 8.49 \times 10^{-3} = 13.58 \text{ kg/m}^2$$

**Step 5: Percentage weight saving** **[1 mark]**

$$W_{saved} = \left(1 - \frac{w_{GRP}}{w_{steel}}\right) \times 100\% = \left(1 - \frac{13.58}{52.67}\right) \times 100\%$$

$$W_{saved} = (1 - 0.258) \times 100\% = 74.2\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{Weight saving} \approx 74\%}$$

**Engineering conclusion:** Switching to GRP gives a **74% weight saving** at the same strength level, even though the GRP plate is **26% thicker**. This dramatic saving is the primary economic driver for composite construction in weight-sensitive applications.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Confusing GRP and FRP definitions** — A surprisingly large number of students write them as synonymous. GRP is a **subset** of FRP; always state this distinction explicitly. **[−1 mark]**
>
> 2. **Using the wrong Rule of Mixtures** — Students frequently apply the **longitudinal** formula to a **transverse** loading problem (or vice versa). Remember: **Voigt (iso-strain, parallel springs) for longitudinal**; **Reuss (iso-stress, series springs) for transverse**. **[−2 marks]**
>
> 3. **Forgetting that $\sigma'_m$ ≠ $\sigma_{mu}$ in the strength ROM** — The matrix stress at fibre failure is **not** the matrix ultimate strength. Use the matrix stress evaluated at the fibre failure strain: $\sigma'_m = E_m \cdot \varepsilon_{fu}$. Many students blindly use the matrix ultimate, which inflates the predicted composite strength. **[−1 to −2 marks]**
>
> 4. **Ignoring the void volume fraction** — A real laminate has $V_v = 1 - 5\%$. Strictly, $V_f + V_m + V_v = 1$. Forgetting voids leads to overestimating $V_f$ and over-predicting modulus. **[−1 mark]**
>
> 5. **Confusing density, weight, and mass** — Density is in kg/m³; weight per unit area is in kg/m² or N/m². Mixing units will lose marks even if the physics is right. **[−0.5 to −1 mark]**
>
> 6. **Stating the Voigt and Reuss bounds without identifying which is upper/lower** — Examiners specifically test this. Voigt ($E_{1L}$) is the **upper bound**; Reuss ($E_{2T}$) is the **lower bound**. **[−1 mark]**
>
> 7. **Not stating the assumptions of the Rule of Mixtures** — Always list: continuous parallel fibres, perfect bonding, no voids, linear elastic constituents, iso-strain or iso-stress as appropriate. **[−1 mark]**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — GRP/FRP for KTU PCNST502 Module 1**

- **GRP ⊂ FRP.** Glass-reinforced plastic is a specific subset of fibre-reinforced plastic. Carbon-fibre composites are FRP but **not** GRP.
- The two phases of a marine composite are: **reinforcement (fibres)** and **matrix (resin)**. Fibres carry tensile load; matrix transfers stress and protects fibres.
- The default marine fibre is **E-glass** (cheap, good strength, density ≈ 2560 kg/m³). Default marine resin is **isophthalic polyester** or **vinyl ester** for hulls; **epoxy** for high-performance / naval.
- **Density of cured marine GRP ≈ 1500 – 1800 kg/m³** — roughly **1/5 of steel** (7850 kg/m³).
- **Rule of Mixtures — Longitudinal (Voigt):** $E_{1L} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ — **upper bound**, iso-strain, valid along fibre direction.
- **Rule of Mixtures — Transverse (Reuss):** $E_{2T} = E_f E_m / (V_f E_m + V_m E_f)$ — **lower bound**, iso-stress, valid perpendicular to fibres.
- **Composite density:** $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ (mass-weighted average).
- **Critical fibre length:** $l_c = \sigma_{fu} d / (2 \tau_i)$. For $l \gg l_c$, fibre reaches its full strength.
- **Fibre efficiency factor:** $\eta_l = 1 - l_c/(2l)$. Approaches 1 for long, continuous fibres.
- **Stiffness usually governs GRP scantling** — not strength. This is opposite to steel design.
- **Sandwich construction** (face skins + low-density core) is preferred for high-performance ship structures because it maximises flexural rigidity per unit weight.
- **Manufacturing processes ranked by $V_f$ quality:** Hand lay-up ($V_f$ 0.20–0.30) < Spray-up (0.15–0.25) < Vacuum infusion/VARTM (0.45–0.55) < Filament winding (0.55–0.65) < Pultrusion (0.50–0.65).
- **Filament winding** is used for **submarine pressure hulls** and cylindrical pressure vessels.
- **Classification society safety factor** for marine GRP: typically **3.0 – 5.0** on ultimate strength.
- **Advantages of GRP over steel for ships:** lightweight, corrosion-free, non-magnetic, design flexibility, low maintenance, good damping, thermal insulator.
- **Disadvantages of GRP:** lower stiffness per unit thickness, fire/smoke/toxicity concerns, difficulty of inspection, poor UV resistance without gel coat, repair is specialised.
- **Specific marine applications:** small craft hulls, naval mine countermeasure vessels (Visby corvette — CFRP), high-speed ferries, superstructures, decks, bulkheads, pipes, funnels, mast sections, propeller blades (CFRP).
- **Void content** should be kept **< 2%** in primary structural laminates — achieved by vacuum infusion, not hand lay-up.
- **Always state assumptions** before applying Rule of Mixtures in KTU exams — this is the single most important habit for scoring full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Ship Framing Systems

A **ship's structural framing system** is the organized arrangement of longitudinal and transverse stiffening members (girders, webs, stringers, frames) attached to the shell plating and decks to maintain the hull's form, resist hydrostatic and hydrodynamic loads, and transmit forces to the primary supporting structures. It is the skeletal framework upon which the entire ship is built, analogous to the ribcage and spine of a biological organism.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1.1):**
> *Framing systems* in ship construction refer to the geometric pattern of primary and secondary stiffeners arranged on shell plating and decks. The three principal classifications are **transverse framing**, **longitudinal framing**, and **combined framing** (also called mixed or ortho­gonal framing). The choice of system governs the structural efficiency, fabrication cost, and operational performance of the vessel.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Skeleton

Think of a ship's hull as a human body:
- The **shell plating** acts as the *skin* (membrane carrying local pressure).
- The **frames and stiffeners** are the *ribs and bones* (carrying bending, shear, and local loads).
- The **keel** is the *spine* (principal longitudinal strength member).
- The **bulkheads** are the *diaphragms* (maintaining shape and providing watertight subdivision).

In a human skeleton, bones are arranged in specific directions to resist loads from gravity, bending, and torsion. Similarly, a ship requires stiffeners in two perpendicular orientations — **longitudinal** (fore-aft) and **transverse** (port-starboard / athwartships) — to resist all six rigid-body modes of motion and the corresponding structural loads.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Framing?**
> Without stiffeners, a flat plate of mild steel would buckle under a hydrostatic pressure of just a few kPa. By introducing closely-spaced stiffeners, the effective panel slenderness (b/t ratio) reduces dramatically, and the buckling resistance multiplies by an order of magnitude.

---

## 1.3 The Three Principal Framing Systems

### 1.3.1 Transverse Framing System (Ring Frame System)

In a **transverse framing system**, the **primary stiffeners (frames) are oriented perpendicular to the ship's centerline**, running from the keel to the deckhead on both sides of the hull. The frames are spaced at regular intervals along the ship's length (typically **500 mm to 1000 mm** apart for merchant ships, depending on size and rule requirements).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** Transverse framing is also called **"ring framing"** because a transverse frame + its associated floor + deck beam forms a closed ring when viewed in a transverse cross-section.

**Characteristics:**
- Frames are the **primary load-bearing members** for local shell pressure.
- Longitudinals (if any) are **secondary**, used only to support the shell plating between frames.
- Spacing of frames (s) is small → panel aspect ratio (s/l) is small.
- Simple to fabricate → lower initial cost.
- Excellent resistance to **local hydrostatic pressure** (head of water in tanks, sea pressure on side shell).

### 1.3.2 Longitudinal Framing System

In a **longitudinal framing system**, the **primary stiffeners (longitudinals) run parallel to the ship's centerline**, closely spaced across the shell plating. The transverse members (frames, web frames, bulkheads) are spaced further apart and act as **secondary supports** for the longitudinals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** Longitudinal framing is the preferred system for large ocean-going vessels because the hull girder bending stresses (which act longitudinally) are carried more efficiently by closely-spaced longitudinal stiffeners aligned with the stress direction.

**Characteristics:**
- Longitudinals are the **primary load-bearing members** for hull girder bending.
- Transverse webs/frames are spaced wider apart (typically **2.5 m to 4.0 m**).
- Reduces the number of transverse cut-outs → better fatigue performance.
- **Higher material efficiency** → 10–20% weight savings over transverse framing.
- Slightly more complex fabrication (more welding, more intersections).

### 1.3.3 Combined Framing System (Mixed Framing)

The **combined framing system** (sometimes called **mixed framing** or **orthogonal framing**) uses **both longitudinal and transverse stiffeners as primary members** in the same region, with the designer selecting the dominant orientation based on local stress patterns. Common variations include:

| Region of Hull | Dominant Primary Direction | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Bottom shell (flat of bottom)** | Longitudinal | High hull girder bending stress |
| **Side shell (amidships)** | Transverse | High local hydrostatic + racking loads |
| **Deck plating** | Longitudinal | Deck compressive buckling critical |
| **Aft end / engine room** | Transverse | Complex local loads, openings |
| **Fore end (bow)** | Transverse | Slamming, wave impact pressures |

> [!NOTE]
> Modern naval architecture typically uses **combined framing** as the default. Pure transverse or pure longitudinal systems are now reserved for specific vessel types (e.g., tugs and small craft → transverse; very large tankers → predominantly longitudinal).

---

## 1.4 Basic Structural Components of a Ship

A modern ship hull consists of the following **primary structural components**, each with a defined load-carrying role:

### 1.4.1 Shell Plating
The outer skin of the ship, formed by rolled steel plates welded edge-to-edge. Forms the watertight envelope. Thickness is determined by classification society rules based on **panel dimensions, design pressure, and material grade** (typically mild steel grade A or higher). Standard thickness: **6 mm to 50 mm** depending on location.

### 1.4.2 Stiffeners (Primary and Secondary)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary Stiffeners:** The principal load-carrying members of a panel. They are typically rolled sections (T-bars, flat bars, L-bars, or bulb plates) directly attached to the plating by welding. They transfer panel loads to the surrounding major structural members (girders, bulkheads, floors, web frames).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Secondary Stiffeners:** Smaller stiffeners (often flat bars) used to subdivide a panel supported by primary stiffeners. They reduce the unsupported span of plating between primary stiffeners, thus increasing local panel buckling resistance. Also called **intercostals** or **sub-stiffeners**.

**Hierarchy of stiffeners** (from largest to smallest):
1. **Girders / Web frames / Stringers** (primary major transverse/longitudinal members)
2. **Primary stiffeners (frames/longitudinals)** (rolled sections attached to plating)
3. **Secondary stiffeners** (intermediate flat bars between primary stiffeners)

### 1.4.3 Bottom Structure
The structural assembly forming the **lower portion of the hull**, including the keel, bottom shell plating, bottom longitudinals/frames, and floors. It must resist:
- Hydrostatic pressure from sea water
- Hull girder bending (sagging → tension at bottom; hogging → compression at bottom in amidships)
- Sloshing loads in ballast/double-bottom tanks
- Local loads from grounding/ship-to-ship contact (bottom slamming)

### 1.4.4 Side Shell Structure
The structural assembly on the **port and starboard sides** of the hull, comprising the side shell plating, side frames/longitudinals, and side stringers. Loads resisted:
- External hydrostatic pressure
- Wave slamming (especially forward)
- Racking loads (transverse shear due to oblique waves)
- Berthing/pontoon impact loads

### 1.4.5 Decks and Superstructure
**Decks** are horizontal plate structures dividing the hull into multiple levels. They act as:
- Longitudinal strength members (effective deck flange of the hull girder)
- Transverse strength members (resist racking)
- Platforms for cargo, machinery, accommodation

**Superstructure** is the decked structure above the uppermost continuous (strength) deck — typically the bridge, accommodation block, and funnel. Superstructures contribute less to hull girder strength but are critical for stability and function.

### 1.4.6 Bulkheads
**Bulkheads** are vertical plate structures that subdivide the hull transversely or longitudinally. Types:
- **Watertight bulkheads** (WT): prevent flooding propagation; required by SOLAS.
- **Non-watertight (dust) bulkheads**: subdivide accommodation/cargo spaces.
- **Collision bulkhead** (forward WT bulkhead).
- **Aft peak bulkhead** (separates machinery/sea).
- **Engine room bulkheads** (forward and aft).
- **Longitudinal bulkheads** (in tankers — separate port/starboard tanks).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** A **collision bulkhead** must be fitted at a position not less than 5% or more than 15% of the rule length (L) abaft the forward perpendicular (FP) for cargo ships, per SOLAS Chapter II-1.

### 1.4.7 Fore End and Aft End Structures
The **fore end** (bow region) is reinforced against:
- Wave slamming (highest hydrodynamic pressures in ship — peak pressures can exceed **350 kPa** in extreme conditions).
- Impact with floating debris, ice, or during berthing.
- Bow thruster foundations (if fitted).

The **aft end** (stern region) is reinforced against:
- Propeller/rudder induced vibration and loads.
- Stern bearing/seal reactions.
- Slamming in head seas (aft shoulder region).
- Accommodation of main engines, shaft brackets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU 2024):**
> - Mild steel yield stress: $\sigma_y = 235 \text{ MPa}$ (Grade A)
> - Higher tensile steel: $\sigma_y = 315 \text{ MPa}$ or $355 \text{ MPa}$ (Grade AH32, AH36, DH36, EH36)
> - Typical frame spacing (transverse system): $s = 600 \text{ mm}$ to $800 \text{ mm}$
> - Typical longitudinal spacing (longitudinal system): $s = 600 \text{ mm}$ to $900 \text{ mm}$
> - Frame spacing (combined system): primary $s_1 = 2.5 \text{ m}$ to $3.5 \text{ m}$; secondary $s_2 = 600 \text{ mm}$ to $800 \text{ mm}$
> - Web frame spacing: $L_w = 2.0 \text{ m}$ to $4.0 \text{ m}$
> - Superstructure freeboard: 0.8 m to 2.0 m above upper deck
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Comparison of Framing Systems — Engineering Trade-Off

The selection of a framing system is a **multicriteria engineering decision** involving structural efficiency, fabrication economics, operational loads, and classification society rules. The following table summarizes the trade-offs at a level suitable for a 14-mark KTU question:

| Parameter | Transverse Framing | Longitudinal Framing | Combined Framing |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary stiffener orientation** | ⟂ to CL (athwartships) | ∥ to CL (fore-aft) | Both, dominant by region |
| **Primary stiffener spacing** | $s = 0.5$ to $1.0 \text{ m}$ | $s = 0.6$ to $0.9 \text{ m}$ | $s_1 = 2.5$ to $4.0 \text{ m}$; $s_2 = 0.6$ to $0.8 \text{ m}$ |
| **Hull girder efficiency** | Lower (~85%) | Higher (~95–100%) | Region-optimized |
| **Material weight** | Heavier | Lighter (10–20% savings) | Intermediate |
| **Local pressure resistance** | Excellent | Adequate (needs more web frames) | Excellent (region-tuned) |
| **Fabrication complexity** | Lower | Higher | Highest |
| **Number of welding passes** | Fewer | More | Most |
| **Fatigue performance** | Good | Better (fewer transverse cut-outs) | Good |
| **Torsional rigidity** | Lower | Higher | High |
| **Best suited for** | Small ships, tankers (older), tugs, naval | VLCCs, ULCCs, bulk carriers, container ships | Modern multi-purpose vessels, naval combatants |

---

## 2.2 The Hull Girder Concept — Why Longitudinal Framing Wins for Large Ships

A ship's hull behaves as a **box girder** of enormous length-to-depth ratio (L/D ≈ 10 to 20). The principal loads are:
- **Vertical bending moment** $M_v$ (sagging in still water + wave)
- **Horizontal bending moment** $M_h$ (from oblique waves)
- **Shear forces** $Q$ (vertical and horizontal)
- **Torsional moment** $T$ (from asymmetric loading and oblique waves)

The **hull girder bending stress** at any point in the cross-section is given by:

$$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot z}{I}$$

where:
- $M$ = vertical bending moment (N·m)
- $z$ = vertical distance from the neutral axis (m)
- $I$ = second moment of area of the hull cross-section about the neutral axis (m⁴)

> [!NOTE]
> The **effective longitudinal strength** is provided by the **deck and bottom plating together with their attached longitudinal stiffeners**. The closer the longitudinals are spaced and the larger their section modulus, the higher the effective longitudinal flange area of the hull girder — and hence the lower the maximum bending stress $\sigma$ for a given $M$.

In a **longitudinal framing system**, the deck and bottom have hundreds of closely-spaced longitudinals contributing to the effective flange area. In a **transverse framing system**, only the plating and a few widely-spaced stringers contribute — so the hull girder is heavier for the same bending strength.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Ship Framing & Structural Mechanics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these equations form the backbone of any 14-mark KTU Ship Structures numerical.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hull girder bending stress | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M \cdot z}{I}$ | $M$: N·m; $z$: m; $I$: m⁴; $\sigma_b$: Pa | Maximum stress in deck/bottom under bending |
| 2 | Hull girder section modulus | $Z = \dfrac{I}{z_{max}}$ | $Z$: m³; $I$: m⁴; $z_{max}$: m | Required $Z$ to keep $\sigma_b \le \sigma_{allow}$ |
| 3 | Hull girder shear stress | $\tau = \dfrac{Q \cdot S}{I \cdot t}$ | $Q$: N; $S$: first moment (m³); $t$: thickness (m) | Maximum shear in side shell |
| 4 | Wave bending moment (empirical) | $M_w = k \cdot L^2 \cdot B \cdot C_b$ | $L$: m; $B$: m; $C_b$: block coefficient; $k \approx 0.019$ (sagging) | Rule-of-thumb still-water + wave bending |
| 5 | Effective plating thickness (panel) | $t_{eff} = t \cdot \sqrt{1 - 0.5 \cdot (\sigma_a / \sigma_{cr})}$ | $t$: m; $\sigma_a$: applied; $\sigma_{cr}$: critical | Reduction for combined loading (DNV/GL) |
| 6 | Panel aspect ratio | $\alpha = \dfrac{s}{l}$ | $s$: short span; $l$: long span | Buckling coefficient lookup |
| 7 | Stiffener slenderness ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{l_{eff}}{i}$ | $l_{eff}$: effective length; $i$: radius of gyration | Column buckling check for stiffener |
| 8 | Hydrostatic pressure (full load) | $p = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (sea water); $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$; $h$: head (m) | Side shell, bottom panel design pressure |
| 9 | Stiffener section modulus requirement | $Z_{req} = \dfrac{M_{stiff}}{m \cdot \sigma_{allow}}$ | $M_{stiff}$: N·mm; $m$: moment reduction factor; $\sigma_{allow}$: MPa | Minimum section modulus of stiffener |
| 10 | Buckling stress (Johnson formula, intermediate) | $\sigma_{cr} = \sigma_y - \dfrac{(\sigma_y \cdot \lambda_0 / \lambda)^2}{4}$ | $\lambda_0 = \pi \sqrt{2E/\sigma_y}$ | Stiffener column buckling in intermediate range |
| 11 | Superstructure freeboard (minimum) | $f_{min} = 0.5 \cdot (L/30) + 0.75$ (empirical) | $L$: m; $f_{min}$: m | Initial design estimate |
| 12 | Collision bulkhead position | $0.05L \le x_c \le 0.15L$ abaft FP | $L$: rule length; $x_c$: distance from FP | SOLAS compliance |
| 13 | Shear area (web frame) | $A_{shear} = d_w \cdot t_w$ | $d_w$: web depth; $t_w$: web thickness | Shear yielding check on web frame |
| 14 | Effective breadth of plating (flange) | $b_{eff} = \min(s, l/3)$ (typical) | $s$: stiffener spacing; $l$: span | Effective flange width for section modulus |
| 15 | Frame span correction factor | $C = 1 - 0.5 \cdot (h_{s}/h_{p})$ | $h_s$: span; $h_p$: pressure head | Account for partial fixity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of These Formulas:**
> - Equations 1–4 govern **global hull girder design** (longitudinal strength).
> - Equations 5–10 govern **local panel and stiffener design** (plating, buckling).
> - Equations 11–12 govern **rule and statutory compliance** (SOLAS, classification).
> - Equations 13–15 govern **secondary member detailing** (web frames, stringers).

---

## 2.4 Load Path in a Ship Structure

Understanding how external loads travel through the structure is essential for KTU 14-mark answers on framing system selection. The **load path** in a typical ocean-going vessel is:

**External loads** (sea pressure, cargo, machinery, waves) 
→ **Shell/Deck plating** (membrane action, distributes pressure locally) 
→ **Primary stiffeners** (frames or longitudinals — carry panel loads) 
→ **Secondary supporting members** (girders, stringers, web frames, floors — collect stiffener reactions) 
→ **Primary supporting members** (transverse bulkheads, large web frames, double bottom) 
→ **Hull girder** (overall bending/shear/torsion) 
→ **Transmitted to sea** via wave-induced motions and resistance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The framing system determines the geometry of the "rungs" in this load ladder. **Longitudinal framing** shortens the first few rungs (plating → long'l → web frame) but extends the bottom of the ladder; **transverse framing** does the opposite. The optimal design matches the stiffener orientation to the dominant local stress vector.

---

## 2.5 Web Frames, Girders, Floors, and Stringers — Definitions

> [!NOTE]
> **Web Frame:** A large transverse primary supporting member, typically with a deep web plate (800 mm to 2000 mm deep) and heavy flanges. Replaces several ordinary frames. Spaced every 2–4 web frame spaces along the ship's length.

> [!NOTE]
> **Floor (Bottom Transverse):** A transverse plate girder in the bottom of the ship, with a vertical web plate running from the keel to the margin plate. In longitudinally framed ships, floors are **light** (intercostal type); in transversely framed ships, floors are **heavy primary** members.

> [!NOTE]
> **Girder:** A longitudinal or transverse primary supporting member with a deep web. In the bottom, longitudinal girders are called **bottom girders** (or centerline girder if on CL). In decks, deck girders are the longitudinal reinforcement of the deck plating.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stringer:** A horizontal longitudinal member on the side shell, providing additional support to the frames and serving as a connection between the side shell and a deck. The **sheer strake** is the topmost strake of the side shell, often reinforced as a stringer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Margin Plate:** The outer edge plate of the double bottom, connecting the bottom shell to the inner bottom. Serves as a longitudinal strength member and as a tank boundary in ballast tanks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hull Girder Bending Stress and Section Modulus Requirement

**Problem statement:** A ship of length $L = 150 \text{ m}$, breadth $B = 22 \text{ m}$, depth $D = 14 \text{ m}$ is subjected to a maximum wave-induced sagging bending moment of $M_s = 8.0 \times 10^8 \text{ N·m}$ at midships. The neutral axis is located at $z_{NA} = 7.0 \text{ m}$ above the keel. The allowable bending stress is $\sigma_{allow} = 175 \text{ MPa}$. Find:
1. The required minimum section modulus $Z$ at deck and bottom.
2. The maximum bending stress in the side shell at a point $z = 4.0 \text{ m}$ above the keel.

**Step 1: Recall the fundamental hull girder bending stress equation.**
The bending stress in any longitudinal element of the hull is given by:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{M \cdot z}{I}$$

where $z$ is measured from the neutral axis (NA).

**Step 2: Define the section modulus.**
The section modulus $Z$ about the NA is defined as:

$$Z = \frac{I}{z_{max}}$$

where $z_{max}$ is the maximum distance from NA to the extreme fiber (top of deck or bottom of keel).

**Step 3: Combine (1) and (2) to get the design equation.**
The maximum bending stress occurs at $z = z_{max}$:

$$\sigma_{b,max} = \frac{M}{Z} \le \sigma_{allow}$$

**Step 4: Rearrange for required section modulus.**

$$Z_{req} = \frac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{allow}}$$

**Step 5: Substitute numerical values.**

$$Z_{req} = \frac{8.0 \times 10^8 \text{ N·m}}{175 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2}$$

$$Z_{req} = 4.571 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 6: Convert to standard units (cm³ for ship design).**

$$Z_{req} = 4.571 \times 10^6 \text{ cm}^3 = 4.571 \times 10^6 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 7: Compute bending stress at $z = 4.0 \text{ m}$ above keel.**
First, find the distance of the point from the neutral axis. The NA is at $z_{NA} = 7.0 \text{ m}$ above the keel, so the point at 4.0 m above the keel lies *below* the NA:

$$z_{point} = z_{NA} - 4.0 = 7.0 - 4.0 = 3.0 \text{ m below NA}$$

The magnitude of $z$ (distance from NA) is $\vert z \vert = 3.0 \text{ m}$. We need the second moment of area $I$ to compute the stress. From $Z_{req}$ and the known $z_{max}$ (we take $z_{max} = D - z_{NA} = 14 - 7 = 7 \text{ m}$):

$$I = Z \cdot z_{max} = 4.571 \times 7.0 = 32.0 \text{ m}^4$$

Then:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{M \cdot \vert z \vert}{I} = \frac{8.0 \times 10^8 \times 3.0}{32.0}$$

$$\sigma_b = 7.5 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} = 75 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 8: Verify the sign convention.** In sagging (M positive), the deck is in compression (top fiber) and the bottom is in tension (bottom fiber). The point at 4.0 m above the keel is below the NA → also in tension. Stress is tensile → +75 MPa. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** The maximum stress of 175 MPa occurs at the deck and bottom. The side shell, being closer to the NA, sees much lower bending stress (75 MPa here). This is why **longitudinal stiffeners in the deck and bottom are critical** — they carry the largest bending stresses in the hull.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Design of a Bottom Panel under Hydrostatic Pressure

**Problem statement:** A double-bottom tank is filled with sea water ballast up to a head of $h = 6.0 \text{ m}$ above the bottom shell. The bottom panel between two adjacent bottom longitudinals has a span $s = 0.8 \text{ m}$ and the stiffener spacing between transverse floors is $l = 3.0 \text{ m}$. Find the required plating thickness using the DNV simplified plate-buckling formula.

**Step 1: Compute the design pressure.**
Sea water density: $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$. Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

$$p = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 6.0$$

$$p = 60330 \text{ Pa} \approx 60.33 \text{ kPa}$$

Add a safety/design margin (typical KTU assumption: 1.0× for service pressure, but classification societies may add margin):

$$p_d = 1.0 \times p = 60.33 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2: Compute the panel aspect ratio.**

$$\alpha = \frac{s}{l} = \frac{0.8}{3.0} = 0.267$$

**Step 3: Use the simplified DNV plate-buckling equation for stiffened panel.**
For a simply supported rectangular plate under uniform lateral pressure, the elastic critical buckling stress is:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{k \cdot \pi^2 \cdot E}{12 \cdot (1 - \nu^2)} \cdot \left( \frac{t}{s} \right)^2$$

For steel: $E = 210 \text{ GPa}$, $\nu = 0.3$, so $\dfrac{\pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \approx 1.994 \text{ GPa}$. The buckling coefficient $k$ depends on aspect ratio $\alpha$ and boundary conditions; for $\alpha = 0.267$ with the long edges simply supported and short edges clamped, $k \approx 7.0$ (from DNV tables).

**Step 4: Equate critical stress to allowable (for first iteration).**
We require the applied stress (in MPa) to be less than the critical buckling stress divided by a safety factor $\gamma = 1.5$ (typical):

$$\sigma_{cr} \ge \gamma \cdot \sigma_{applied}$$

For a plate of unit width under pressure $p$, the maximum bending moment per unit width is:

$$M_{max} = \frac{p \cdot s^2}{8} = \frac{60330 \times 0.8^2}{8} = 4826.4 \text{ N·m/m}$$

The corresponding maximum stress in a simply supported plate of thickness $t$ and unit width (section modulus per meter = $t^2/6$ in m·m):

$$\sigma_{applied} = \frac{M_{max}}{t^2/6} = \frac{6 \cdot M_{max}}{t^2}$$

**Step 5: Solve for $t$.**
Set $\sigma_{cr} = \gamma \cdot \sigma_{applied}$:

$$k \cdot \frac{\pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \cdot \left( \frac{t}{s} \right)^2 = \gamma \cdot \frac{6 \cdot p \cdot s^2}{8 \cdot t^2} \cdot (\text{unit width})^{-1}$$

Solving algebraically for $t^4$:

$$t^4 = \frac{\gamma \cdot p \cdot s^4 \cdot 6 \cdot 12 \cdot (1-\nu^2)}{8 \cdot k \cdot \pi^2 \cdot E}$$

**Step 6: Plug in numbers.**
$t^4 = \dfrac{1.5 \times 60330 \times (0.8)^4 \times 6 \times 12 \times (1 - 0.09)}{8 \times 7.0 \times \pi^2 \times 210 \times 10^9}$

Numerator:
$$1.5 \times 60330 \times 0.4096 \times 6 \times 12 \times 0.91 = 1.5 \times 60330 \times 0.4096 \times 65.52$$
$$= 1.5 \times 60330 \times 26.84 = 2.43 \times 10^6$$

Denominator:
$$8 \times 7.0 \times 9.8696 \times 210 \times 10^9 = 8 \times 7.0 \times 9.8696 \times 210 \times 10^9$$
$$= 1.161 \times 10^{14}$$

Therefore:
$$t^4 = \frac{2.43 \times 10^6}{1.161 \times 10^{14}} = 2.093 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^4$$

$$t = (2.093 \times 10^{-8})^{1/4} = 0.01204 \text{ m} = 12.04 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 7: Round up to standard plate thickness.**
Adopt $t = 13 \text{ mm}$ (standard mild steel plate thickness, next size above 12 mm).

**Step 8: Sanity check the deflection.**
Maximum deflection of simply supported plate:

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{5 \cdot p \cdot s^4}{384 \cdot E \cdot I_{plate}} \cdot \text{unit width}$$

$I_{plate} = t^3/12$ per unit width. For $t = 13 \text{ mm}$:
$I_{plate} = (0.013)^3 / 12 = 1.831 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4/\text{m}$

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{5 \times 60330 \times (0.8)^4}{384 \times 210 \times 10^9 \times 1.831 \times 10^{-7}}$$
$$= \frac{5 \times 60330 \times 0.4096}{1.476 \times 10^7} = \frac{1.235 \times 10^5}{1.476 \times 10^7} = 8.37 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$
$$= 8.4 \text{ mm}$$

Deflection/span ratio: $\delta/s = 8.4/800 = 0.0105$ (about 1/95). Acceptable per most rules (limit is usually 1/100 to 1/150).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Take-away for KTU:** For typical bottom panels in a double bottom, plating thickness ends up in the range **10 mm to 20 mm** for commercial ships. The required thickness is highly sensitive to (a) stiffener spacing $s$, (b) design pressure $p$, and (c) yield stress $\sigma_y$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Comparison of Transverse vs Longitudinal Framing for a Specific Hull

**Problem statement:** A bulk carrier has $L = 200 \text{ m}$, $B = 32 \text{ m}$, $D = 18 \text{ m}$, $T = 12 \text{ m}$. Compare the bottom plating thickness required for:
- (a) Transverse framing with frame spacing $s_T = 0.7 \text{ m}$ (bottom frames every 0.7 m, supported by heavy floors every 3.5 m).
- (b) Longitudinal framing with longitudinal spacing $s_L = 0.8 \text{ m}$ (supported by web frames every 3.5 m).

Assume the bottom is loaded by an effective head $h = 4.0 \text{ m}$ (combined still water + dynamic head for bottom in still water). Use the same simplified plate-buckling formula with $k = 7.0$ and $\gamma = 1.5$.

**Step 1: Compute design pressure (same for both).**

$$p = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 4.0 = 40218 \text{ Pa} \approx 40.2 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2: Apply the formula from §3.2.**

$$t^4 = \frac{\gamma \cdot p \cdot s^4 \cdot 6 \cdot 12 \cdot (1-\nu^2)}{8 \cdot k \cdot \pi^2 \cdot E}$$

Substituting constants (with $E = 210 \text{ GPa}$, $\nu = 0.3$, $k = 7.0$, $\gamma = 1.5$):

$$t^4 = \frac{1.5 \times 40218 \times s^4 \times 65.52}{1.161 \times 10^{14}}$$

$$t^4 = \frac{1.5 \times 40218 \times 65.52}{1.161 \times 10^{14}} \times s^4 = \frac{3.953 \times 10^6}{1.161 \times 10^{14}} \times s^4$$

$$t^4 = 3.404 \times 10^{-8} \times s^4$$

**Step 3: Solve for case (a) — Transverse framing, $s = 0.7 \text{ m}$.**

$$t^4 = 3.404 \times 10^{-8} \times (0.7)^4 = 3.404 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.2401 = 8.172 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^4$$

$$t_a = (8.172 \times 10^{-9})^{1/4} = 9.52 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 9.52 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Solve for case (b) — Longitudinal framing, $s = 0.8 \text{ m}$.**

$$t^4 = 3.404 \times 10^{-8} \times (0.8)^4 = 3.404 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.4096 = 1.394 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^4$$

$$t_b = (1.394 \times 10^{-8})^{1/4} = 1.086 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m} = 10.86 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Compare.**

$$\frac{t_b}{t_a} = \frac{10.86}{9.52} = 1.141$$

So longitudinal framing requires **~14% thicker plating** for the same panel load because the longitudinal spacing is slightly larger (0.8 m vs 0.7 m). However, longitudinal framing requires fewer transverse web frames (every 3.5 m is same, but the bottom has many fewer transverse floors since these are now web frames), reducing the total number of transverse structural cuts and improving the **fatigue life** and **overall hull girder efficiency**.

**Step 6: Net weight assessment (qualitative).**
- Transverse framing: many transverse floors (heavy); thinner plate; less efficient hull girder.
- Longitudinal framing: fewer transverse floors; slightly thicker plate; more efficient hull girder; net 10–20% weight savings in bottom structure for typical merchant ships.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Take-away:** The framing choice is not just about plate thickness — it's a **system-level trade-off** involving plate, stiffeners, supporting members, and hull girder efficiency. The longitudinal system wins overall for large ocean-going ships despite needing slightly thicker plate in some local panels.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flowchart for Framing System Selection

| Step | Design Question | Decision Rule | Default Choice |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | What is the rule length L? | L > 150 m → prefer longitudinal or combined | Longitudinal/Combined |
| 2 | What is the principal load? | Sagging/hogging bending dominant → longitudinal | Longitudinal for deck/bottom |
| 3 | Is slamming/pounding critical? | Yes (forward) → transverse in fore end | Combined |
| 4 | Are there many openings (machinery)? | Yes → transverse flexibility | Transverse/Combined |
| 5 | Fabrication cost constraint? | Tight budget → transverse (simpler) | Transverse |
| 6 | Class rules requirement? | Most modern rules assume combined | Combined |
| 7 | Vessel type? | Tanker → longitudinal; Tug → transverse; Naval → combined | By vessel type |

---

## 3.5 Component-by-Component Load Carrying Analysis

### 3.5.1 Bottom Structure
- **Compression** in hogging (midships, mid-L) — bottom in compression → susceptible to **plate buckling** and **stiffener tripping**.
- **Tension** in sagging (midships, mid-L) — bottom in tension → rarely a problem (steel is strong in tension).
- **Local pressure** from sea water + cargo (dry bulk, ballast) → bottom plating must resist combined global + local stresses.

### 3.5.2 Side Shell
- **Shear stress** dominates (hull girder shear Q). Side shell acts as the **web of the hull girder**.
- **Local pressure** from sea water (max at design draft).
- **Slamming** loads in bow region (peak pressure up to 350 kPa).
- **Berthing impact** loads on parallel body.

### 3.5.3 Decks
- **Compression** in sagging → deck in compression → susceptible to **plate buckling** and **stiffener column buckling**.
- **Tension** in hogging → deck in tension → rarely a problem.
- **Local loads** from cargo, equipment, personnel.

### 3.5.4 Bulkheads
- **Transverse loads** from hydrostatic pressure (especially in partly-filled tanks).
- **Shear stiffness** contribution to hull transverse strength.
- **Crashworthiness** (collision bulkhead must absorb impact energy).

### 3.5.5 Fore End
- **Slamming pressure** in head seas — peak pressures of $p = 350 \text{ kPa}$ over a slamming area.
- **Green water impact** loads on bow flare.
- **Ice strengthening** (if classed for ice).
- **Impact loads** during berthing (bow thruster, anchor).

### 3.5.6 Aft End
- **Vibration** from propeller excitation → avoid resonance in side shell natural frequency.
- **Stern bearing reaction** → localized reinforcement.
- **Slamming** in head seas at aft shoulder.
- **Rudders, shaft brackets, A-brackets** → heavy local reinforcement.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**Q: Differentiate between primary and secondary stiffeners in a ship's hull. Give one example of each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Primary stiffeners** are the main load-bearing members of a structural panel, directly attached to the shell or deck plating. They carry the panel loads and transfer them to the secondary supporting members (girders, web frames, floors). They are typically rolled sections such as **T-bars, bulb plates, or L-bars** of substantial size. *Example:* A bottom frame in a transverse framing system — a T-section 200×10+100×12 mm welded to the bottom plating.
>
> **Secondary stiffeners** are smaller flat-bar stiffeners fitted between primary stiffeners to subdivide the panel and reduce the unsupported span of plating, thereby increasing local panel buckling resistance. They are typically flat bars like **100×10 mm** or **150×12 mm**. *Example:* A flat-bar sub-stiffener fitted between two bottom longitudinals in a longitudinally framed ship.
>
> *(Mark split: 1 mark for primary definition + example, 1 mark for secondary definition + example, 1 mark for clear differentiation.)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

**Q: State and briefly explain any three types of bulkheads used in a ship.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> 1. **Collision Bulkhead** (1 mark): A watertight transverse bulkhead fitted at $0.05L$ to $0.15L$ abaft the forward perpendicular (per SOLAS). It prevents flooding of the forward spaces in case of bow collision damage.
>
> 2. **Engine Room Bulkheads** (1 mark): Watertight transverse bulkheads fitted at the forward and aft ends of the machinery space. They contain flooding, oil spills, and fire within the engine room.
>
> 3. **Longitudinal Bulkhead** (1 mark): A vertical bulkhead running fore-aft, used in tankers to separate port and starboard cargo/ballast tanks, providing damage stability and limiting free-surface effects.
>
> *(Alternative acceptable answers: Aft peak bulkhead, wash bulkhead, swash bulkhead, fire-resisting bulkhead, deep tank bulkhead, cofferdam bulkhead.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: (a) Describe the three principal framing systems used in ship construction with neat sketches. Compare their advantages and disadvantages. (7 marks)**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Introduction (1 mark):** A ship's framing system is the organized arrangement of primary and secondary stiffeners attached to the shell and deck plating to maintain hull form and resist applied loads. The three principal systems are transverse, longitudinal, and combined framing.
>
> **1. Transverse Framing System (2 marks):**
> In this system, the **primary stiffeners are frames oriented perpendicular to the centerline**, typically spaced at **0.5 to 1.0 m** apart. The frames, together with associated floors and deck beams, form closed "ring" cross-sections. Longitudinals (if any) act as secondary members supporting the shell between frames.
>
> *Sketch (description):* A transverse cross-section of the hull shows multiple T-frames spaced 600–800 mm apart vertically rising from the bottom to the deckhead, with a heavy floor at the bottom of each ring.
>
> **Advantages:** Simple to fabricate, lower initial cost, excellent local pressure resistance, fewer cut-outs in shell.
> **Disadvantages:** Heavier hull girder, lower material efficiency, more welding intersections, less efficient at resisting global bending.
>
> **2. Longitudinal Framing System (2 marks):**
> In this system, **primary stiffeners (longitudinals) run parallel to the centerline**, typically spaced at **0.6 to 0.9 m**. The transverse supporting members (web frames, bulkheads) are spaced wider, every 2.5–4.0 m.
>
> *Sketch (description):* A bottom panel view shows closely-spaced longitudinals (every 700 mm) running fore-aft, with widely-spaced web frames (every 3 m) providing intermediate support.
>
> **Advantages:** Higher hull girder efficiency, 10–20% material weight savings, better fatigue performance, higher torsional rigidity.
> **Disadvantages:** More complex fabrication, requires precise alignment, more welding, slightly more expensive.
>
> **3. Combined Framing System (1 mark):**
> Uses both orientations as primary, selecting the dominant direction by region. Bottom and deck → longitudinal; side shell → transverse. Most modern vessels use this hybrid system as the default.
>
> **Conclusion (1 mark):** The selection of framing system depends on vessel size, type, operational profile, and class rules. Combined framing is the most common in modern shipbuilding.

---

**(b) A double-bottom tank is subjected to a hydrostatic head of $5.5 \text{ m}$ of sea water. The bottom plating has a panel of dimensions $0.85 \text{ m} \times 3.2 \text{ m}$ (s × l). Using a buckling coefficient $k = 7.0$, $\gamma = 1.5$, $E = 210 \text{ GPa}$, $\nu = 0.3$, find the required plate thickness $t$. (7 marks)**

> **Model Solution (7 marks):**
>
> **Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute design pressure.
> $$p = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 5.5 = 55,289 \text{ Pa} = 55.29 \text{ kPa}$$
>
> **Step 2 (1 mark):** State the critical buckling stress equation.
> $$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{k \cdot \pi^2 \cdot E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \cdot \left(\frac{t}{s}\right)^2$$
>
> **Step 3 (1 mark):** State the applied stress from bending of plate as simply supported.
> $$M_{max} = \frac{p \cdot s^2}{8} \quad \text{(per unit width)}$$
> $$\sigma_{applied} = \frac{6 M_{max}}{t^2} = \frac{6 p s^2}{8 t^2} = \frac{3 p s^2}{4 t^2}$$
>
> **Step 4 (1 mark):** Apply safety criterion: $\sigma_{cr} = \gamma \cdot \sigma_{applied}$
> $$\frac{k \pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \cdot \frac{t^2}{s^2} = \gamma \cdot \frac{3 p s^2}{4 t^2}$$
>
> **Step 5 (1 mark):** Solve for $t^4$:
> $$t^4 = \frac{\gamma \cdot 3 p s^4 \cdot 12(1-\nu^2)}{4 \cdot k \pi^2 E}$$
> $$t^4 = \frac{1.5 \times 3 \times 55,289 \times (0.85)^4 \times 12 \times 0.91}{4 \times 7.0 \times \pi^2 \times 210 \times 10^9}$$
>
> **Step 6 (1 mark):** Numerator:
> $1.5 \times 3 = 4.5$
> $4.5 \times 55,289 = 248,800.5$
> $(0.85)^4 = 0.5220$
> $12 \times 0.91 = 10.92$
> Numerator = $248,800.5 \times 0.5220 \times 10.92 = 1.418 \times 10^6$
>
> **Step 7 (1 mark):** Denominator:
> $4 \times 7.0 \times 9.8696 \times 210 \times 10^9 = 5.804 \times 10^{13}$
>
> $t^4 = 1.418 \times 10^6 / 5.804 \times 10^{13} = 2.443 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^4$
>
> $t = (2.443 \times 10^{-8})^{1/4} = 0.01251 \text{ m} = 12.51 \text{ mm}$
>
> **Final answer:** Adopt standard plate thickness $t = 13 \text{ mm}$ (next size above 12.5 mm).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q: (a) Explain the different structural components of a ship's bottom structure. With a sketch, describe the double bottom arrangement. (7 marks)**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Introduction (0.5 mark):** The bottom structure of a ship comprises all the structural members forming the lower envelope of the hull, from the keel to the lower turn of the bilge. It carries hydrostatic pressure, hull girder bending loads, sloshing loads in tanks, and grounding/berthing impact loads.
>
> **Components (4 marks):**
>
> 1. **Bottom Shell Plating** (0.5 mark): The outer skin of the bottom, made of rolled steel plates welded together. Thickness 14–22 mm for typical merchant ships. Stiffened by either transverse frames or longitudinals.
>
> 2. **Keel** (0.5 mark): The centerline bottom plate or rolled section, providing longitudinal strength at the bottom of the hull. Types: flat-plate keel, bar keel, duct keel.
>
> 3. **Centerline Girder** (0.5 mark): A vertical plate girder running fore-aft on the centerline, providing longitudinal strength and a boundary for double-bottom tanks. In tankers, often a duct keel.
>
> 4. **Side Girders (Bottom Girders)** (0.5 mark): Longitudinal plate girders running fore-aft, parallel to the centerline girder, providing support to the bottom longitudinals and acting as tank boundaries.
>
> 5. **Bottom Floors** (0.5 mark): Transverse plate girders at the bottom, connecting the centerline girder to the margin plates. Heavy in transverse framing, light/intercostal in longitudinal framing.
>
> 6. **Margin Plate** (0.5 mark): The outermost strake of the inner bottom, connecting the double bottom to the side shell. Provides longitudinal strength and tank boundary.
>
> 7. **Inner Bottom Plating (Tank Top)** (0.5 mark): The inner skin of the double bottom, separating ballast/cargo tanks from the cargo hold or machinery space.
>
> 8. **Bottom Longitudinals / Frames** (0.5 mark): Closely-spaced primary stiffeners attached to the bottom shell plating. T-bars, bulb plates, or L-bars, spaced 600–800 mm.
>
> **Double Bottom Sketch (description) (1.5 marks):**
> A transverse cross-section of a double bottom shows:
> - Outer bottom shell (top of section in cross-section view)
> - Inner bottom (tank top) at the level of approximately $h/3$ from the keel
> - Centerline girder running vertically at the center
> - Two side girders parallel to the centerline
> - Floors (transverse plates) at regular intervals
> - Margin plates at the edges connecting inner bottom to side shell
> - The space between outer and inner bottoms is divided into port, center, and starboard tanks
>
> **Conclusion (1 mark):** The double bottom serves multiple purposes: structural strength, subdivision for damage stability, ballast capacity, fuel oil storage, and provides a clean working surface above the tank top.

---

**(b) Define a longitudinal framing system. A 12,000 DWT bulk carrier has $L = 130 \text{ m}$, $B = 20 \text{ m}$, $D = 11 \text{ m}$. The maximum wave-induced bending moment is $M_w = 4.5 \times 10^8 \text{ N·m}$ and the neutral axis is at $5.0 \text{ m}$ above the keel. The required section modulus at the deck is $Z_D = 3.0 \text{ m}^3$ and at the bottom is $Z_B = 3.2 \text{ m}^3$. Compute: (i) the maximum bending stress in the deck, (ii) the maximum bending stress in the bottom shell, and (iii) the bending stress in the side shell at a point $2.5 \text{ m}$ above the keel. (7 marks)**

> **Model Solution (7 marks):**
>
> **Step 1 (1 mark):** State the hull girder bending stress equation.
> $$\sigma_b = \frac{M \cdot z}{I} = \frac{M}{Z}$$
> where $Z$ is the section modulus to the point of interest.
>
> **Step 2 (1.5 marks):** (i) Maximum bending stress in the deck.
> The required section modulus at the deck is $Z_D = 3.0 \text{ m}^3$.
> $$\sigma_{deck} = \frac{M_w}{Z_D} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^8}{3.0} = 1.5 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} = 150 \text{ MPa}$$
>
> **Step 3 (1.5 marks):** (ii) Maximum bending stress in the bottom shell.
> $$\sigma_{bottom} = \frac{M_w}{Z_B} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^8}{3.2} = 1.406 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} \approx 140.6 \text{ MPa}$$
>
> **Step 4 (1.5 marks):** (iii) Bending stress at 2.5 m above the keel.
> Distance of point from neutral axis:
> $$z = \vert z_{NA} - 2.5 \vert = \vert 5.0 - 2.5 \vert = 2.5 \text{ m (below NA)}$$
>
> We need $I$ to compute stress at this point. Use the bottom section modulus:
> $$I = Z_B \cdot z_{NA} = 3.2 \times 5.0 = 16.0 \text{ m}^4$$
>
> (Note: $z_{max}$ at the bottom = 5.0 m, which is the distance from NA to the bottom keel — we assume the bottom shell is at the keel level for this problem.)
>
> Then:
> $$\sigma_{side} = \frac{M_w \cdot \vert z \vert}{I} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^8 \times 2.5}{16.0} = 7.031 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} \approx 70.3 \text{ MPa}$$
>
> **Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Interpretation.
> - Deck stress (150 MPa) is the largest, confirming the deck is the critical tension/compression fiber in the hull girder.
> - Bottom stress (140.6 MPa) is slightly less because $Z_B$ is larger (more material at the bottom in this design).
> - Side shell stress (70.3 MPa) is roughly half of the deck stress, because the point is closer to the NA. The side shell primarily carries shear, not bending.
> - All stresses are within typical allowable limits for mild steel ($\sigma_y = 235 \text{ MPa}$).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MARK-LOSS PITFALLS — KTU 2024 Board Evaluation Pattern**
>
> **Pitfall 1 — Confusing stiffener hierarchies (–2 to –3 marks):** Many students interchange "primary stiffener" and "secondary stiffener" or fail to distinguish them from "girders" and "web frames." KTU examiners specifically test the **hierarchy of structural members** (Plate → Secondary stiffener → Primary stiffener → Girder/Frame → Bulkhead). Memorize the order.
>
> **Pitfall 2 — Forgetting units in numerical problems (–2 marks):** A 14-mark numerical typically deducts 1–2 marks for missing or wrong units. Always write: $p$ in Pa, $M$ in N·m, $Z$ in m³, $I$ in m⁴, $\sigma$ in Pa (or MPa). Convert at the end with explicit factor.
>
> **Pitfall 3 — Sketches without labels (–2 to –3 marks):** A "neat sketch" question expects labeled components (frame, longitudinal, floor, girder, plate, bulkhead). An unlabeled diagram earns at most 1 mark even if drawn correctly. Always include component names.
>
> **Pitfall 4 — Mixing up framing system characteristics (–2 marks):** In comparative questions, students often claim "transverse framing is heavier than longitudinal" without quantifying or justifying. The correct justification: transverse → more material in supporting members; longitudinal → more material efficiency in hull girder; net weight savings 10–20%.
>
> **Pitfall 5 — Not stating assumptions (–1 to –2 marks):** When solving buckling or bending problems, always state: (a) boundary conditions (simply supported/clamped), (b) safety factor, (c) material properties, (d) direction of loading. KTU examiners look for these.
>
> **Pitfall 6 — Forgetting SOLAS/Class rule references (–1 mark):** When discussing bulkheads (collision, engine room), always reference **SOLAS Chapter II-1** and the **0.05L–0.15L rule** for collision bulkhead position. This single reference can earn 1 easy mark.
>
> **Pitfall 7 — Wrong direction of stress (–1 mark):** In sagging/hogging, deck and bottom stresses are in opposite signs. Always state the condition (sagging or hogging) before computing the stress.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 1, Ship Structures I (KTU 2024)**
>
> ### A. Framing Systems (3 types)
> - **Transverse:** Frames ⟂ to CL, spacing 0.5–1.0 m. Best for: tugs, small craft. Heavy, simple.
> - **Longitudinal:** Long'ls ∥ to CL, spacing 0.6–0.9 m. Web frames every 2.5–4.0 m. Best for: VLCCs, bulkers, containers. Light, efficient.
> - **Combined:** Hybrid. Best for: modern ships, naval. Default choice today.
>
> ### B. Stiffener Hierarchy (Plate → Top Member)
> 1. **Plating** (t = 6–30 mm)
> 2. **Secondary stiffeners** (flat bars, 100×10 to 200×12 mm)
> 3. **Primary stiffeners** (T-bars, bulb plates, L-bars)
> 4. **Girders, web frames, stringers, floors** (deep-web plate members)
> 5. **Bulkheads, double bottom** (major subdivision)
>
> ### C. Structural Components
> - **Bottom:** Bottom plating, keel, centerline girder, side girders, floors, margin plates, inner bottom, bottom long'ls/frames
> - **Side:** Side plating, frames/long'ls, side stringers, sheer strake
> - **Decks:** Deck plating, deck long'ls/beam, deck girders, hatch coamings
> - **Superstructure:** Bridge deck, accommodation, bulwarks
> - **Bulkheads:** Collision, engine room, longitudinal, transverse, wash, swash, deep tank
> - **Fore end:** Stem, bow long'ls, slamming reinforcement, hawse pipes
> - **Aft end:** Stern frame, A-bracket, rudder, transom, propeller boss
>
> ### D. Key Formulae
> - Bending stress: $\sigma = Mz/I = M/Z$
> - Shear stress: $\tau = QS/(It)$
> - Pressure: $p = \rho g h$ (sea: $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$)
> - Plate buckling: $\sigma_{cr} \propto (t/s)^2$
> - Aspect ratio: $\alpha = s/l$
> - SOLAS collision BH: $0.05L \le x_c \le 0.15L$ abaft FP
> - Frame spacing (transverse): 0.5–1.0 m
> - Long'l spacing: 0.6–0.9 m
> - Web frame spacing: 2.5–4.0 m
>
> ### E. Class Notes
> - Mild steel Grade A: $\sigma_y = 235 \text{ MPa}$
> - Higher tensile: AH32 = 315 MPa, AH36 = 355 MPa
> - Bow slamming peak pressure: 250–350 kPa
> - Bottom structure forms bottom flange of hull girder
> - Deck forms top flange of hull girder
> - Side shell forms web of hull girder
> - Neutral axis typically at 0.45D to 0.55D above keel
> - Section modulus: deck and bottom contribute ~80–90% of total
> - Longitudinal framing gives 10–20% weight savings
> - Combined framing is industry default for modern vessels
>
> ### F. SOLAS / Statutory Compliance
> - Collision bulkhead: 0.05L to 0.15L abaft FP
> - Number of transverse WT bulkheads: at least 4 (forepeak BH, collision BH, engine room BHs fore & aft, aftpeak BH)
> - Double bottom: required for cargo ships > 500 GT per MARPOL
> - Engine room surrounded by WT bulkheads fore & aft
> - Subdivision load line marks: subdivide ship into floodable compartments
>
> ### G. Common Exam Traps
> - Don't confuse "primary stiffener" with "girder"
> - Don't claim longitudinal framing has "thinner" plate always — it depends on spacing
> - Don't forget that side stringers reduce frame span and improve buckling resistance
> - Don't say "transverse framing is best" — say "transverse framing is preferred for X type of vessel"
> - Always state the loading condition (sagging/hogging) before computing bending stress
> - Always convert units explicitly: $1 \text{ MPa} = 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 1 \text{ N/mm}^2$
