# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**"List and briefly explain any three considerations in ship structural design."**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

The three key considerations in ship structural design are:

1. **Functional Considerations:** The vessel type, cargo, route, service life, and operational envelope (e.g., a 25-year-old bulk carrier plying the Indian Ocean faces corrosion and fatigue loads fundamentally different from a 50-year-old chemical tanker in the Baltic).

2. **Regulatory Considerations:** The ship must comply with the rules of a recognized Classification Society (IRS, LR, DNV, ABS) and statutory codes (SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Line). Class rules provide minimum scantlings.

3. **Strength Considerations:** The structure must resist global bending (hull girder), shear, torsion, local pressure, buckling, and fatigue. Both **allowable stress** and **ultimate strength** checks must be performed.

(1 mark per consideration, with 1 mark for accurate definition/keyword usage.)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]**

**"What is a shell expansion drawing? Why is it prepared?"**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

A **Shell Expansion Drawing** (also called Shell Plate Development or Plate Layout) is a 2D drawing that shows the curved surfaces of the ship's hull **unfolded into flat plates**, with every plate's outline, dimensions, plate number, position, and orientation marked.

It is prepared because:
1. The hull is a 3D doubly-curved surface (for most ships) but steel plates are flat. The drawing gives **fabrication instructions** to the shipyard.
2. It enables **cutting plan generation** and **plate nesting** to minimize wastage.
3. It acts as a **quality reference** during steel cutting, bending, and welding.

(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for any two reasons.)

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### ⭐ **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU ESE – Module 3, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **loads acting on a ship** during its service life. Classify them as global and local loads with examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A bulk carrier has the following data:
- Length L = 140 m, Breadth B = 22 m, Draught T = 9.5 m, Block Coefficient $C_b$ = 0.72
- Service speed = 14 knots, Mild Steel grade A, σ_y = 235 N/mm², Safety Factor SF = 1.43
- Wave bending moment coefficient (LR) k = 0.110 for hogging and 0.085 for sagging

Compute the **required section modulus** of the midship section and **permissible bending stress**. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks**

**Sketch:** A ship floating in waves (sagging and hogging conditions), with arrows showing weight (W), buoyancy (B), wave crest, wave trough, cargo load arrows, and slamming impact.

**Classification of Loads:**

| Global Loads | Cause |
|---|---|
| Still Water Bending Moment (M_s) | Weight + buoyancy at rest |
| Wave Bending Moment – Hogging (M_w^hog) | Trough at midship |
| Wave Bending Moment – Sagging (M_w^sag) | Crest at midship |
| Wave Shear Force (F) | Wave differential pressure |
| Torsional Moment (T) | Oblique seas |
| Dynamic (impact) loads from waves | Slamming, bow emergence |

| Local Loads | Cause |
|---|---|
| Hydrostatic pressure on shell | Water column on plating |
| Slamming pressure (forward bottom) | Wave impact on flat bottom |
| Cargo pressure (grain, bulk) | Outward force on hold walls |
| Tank pressure (ballast, oil) | Liquid pressure on bulkheads |
| Deck loads (containers, vehicles) | Local pressure on deck |
| Berthing and mooring loads | Fender reactions |

(4 marks for classification + 3 marks for sketch and examples.)

#### **Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1:** Compute still water and wave bending moments.

For a typical bulk carrier:
$$
M_s \approx 0.05 L^2 B C_b \quad (\text{LR empirical})
$$
$$
M_s = 0.05 \times 140^2 \times 22 \times 0.72 = 0.05 \times 19600 \times 22 \times 0.72
$$
$$
M_s = 0.05 \times 19600 \times 15.84 = 15523 \text{ kN·m} \approx 15.5 \text{ MN·m}
$$

Wave bending moment (hogging, governing for many bulk carriers):
$$
M_w^{hog} = k L^2 B C_b \times 10^{-3} \text{ (MN·m, with L in m, B in m)}
$$

Using k = 0.110:
$$
M_w^{hog} = 0.110 \times 140^2 \times 22 \times 0.72 \times 10^{-3}
$$
$$
M_w^{hog} = 0.110 \times 19600 \times 15.84 \times 10^{-3} = 34.16 \text{ MN·m}
$$

**Step 2:** Total design bending moment.
$$
M_{total} = M_s + M_w^{hog} = 15.5 + 34.16 = 49.66 \text{ MN·m}
$$

**Step 3:** Permissible bending stress.
$$
\sigma_{perm} = \frac{\sigma_y}{SF} = \frac{235}{1.43} = 164.3 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 4:** Required section modulus.
$$
Z_{req} = \frac{M_{total}}{\sigma_{perm}} = \frac{49.66 \times 10^6 \text{ N·m}}{164.3 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2}
$$
$$
Z_{req} = 0.302 \text{ m}^3 = 3.02 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^3
$$

> [!NOTE]
> If KTU class uses different k-coefficient tables, students should use the formula given in the question paper. The format above is general.

(2 marks: M_total, 1 mark: σ_perm, 2 marks: Z_req, 2 marks: final answer with units.)

---

### ⭐ **Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU ESE – Module 3, 14 Marks, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **step-by-step procedure for the development of a shell expansion drawing** from a given lines plan. List any four important items that must be shown on the drawing. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For an assigned vessel of length L = 120 m, breadth B = 18 m, depth D = 12 m, with side shell thickness 16 mm and deck plating 18 mm, treat the midship section as a **rectangular box girder** for a first-pass estimate. Compute the **moment of inertia** about the horizontal neutral axis (centroid at depth/2) and the **section modulus**. State if it meets a required section modulus of 2.5 m³. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks**

**Procedure for Shell Expansion Drawing:**

1. **Read the Offset Table** from the lines plan to obtain half-breadths at every frame station, waterline, and buttock line.

2. **Identify Frame Lines** as vertical reference lines spaced typically at 600 mm or as per the frame spacing plan.

3. **Mark Waterlines (WL)** at regular intervals (e.g., every 1 m) from baseline (WL 0) to sheer line.

4. **Mark Buttocks** at regular intervals (e.g., every 2 m) from centerline (B0) to side (B ½B).

5. **Divide the hull surface** into rectangular plate panels bounded by frame lines and waterlines (or buttocks).

6. **Develop each plate** onto a flat plane using:
   - **Parallel line development** (for developable surfaces)
   - **Triangulation** (for non-developable surfaces like bilge area)
   - **Computer flattening** (using Rhino, AVEVA, or NAPA)

7. **Add edge margins** for welding (typically 25–50 mm on each plate edge).

8. **Mark plate identification** — Plate Number, Plate Position (e.g., "B2-P4-S"), thickness, material grade, and orientation arrows.

9. **Plot the developed plates** to scale on a drawing sheet (typically 1:50 or 1:100).

10. **Add Title Block**, Scale, Plate Schedule Table, Notes (e.g., "All plates to be shot-blasted SA 2.5 before priming").

**Items to be shown:**
1. Plate boundaries and dimensions
2. Frame line positions
3. Waterline and buttock intersections
4. Plate number and position code
5. Thickness and material grade
6. Welding edge preparation details
7. Curvature and bending instructions
8. Centerline and butt line references

(3 marks for procedure steps, 2 marks for any 4 items, 2 marks for neatness/sketch.)

#### **Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1:** Effective section dimensions:
- Breadth B = 18 m → two side shell plates, each **9 m wide**
- Depth D = 12 m → two side shell plates, each **12 m tall**
- Deck thickness t_d = 18 mm = 0.018 m
- Bottom (assumed equivalent) t_b = 16 mm = 0.016 m

**Step 2:** Approximation — since the section is symmetric about both axes, **NA is at D/2 = 6 m from baseline**.

**Step 3:** Moment of inertia using parallel axis theorem.

For a rectangular plate strip:
$$I = \frac{b t^3}{12} + A \bar{z}^2$$

Treat side shell plates as two **vertical strips** each of:
- Width (perpendicular to bending axis) b = 0.016 m
- Height (along bending axis) h = 12 m
- Area A = 0.016 × 12 = 0.192 m²

Centroid at mid-height, so $\bar{z}$ from NA = 0. Self second moment only:
$$I_{side,each} = \frac{0.016 \times 12^3}{12} = \frac{0.016 \times 1728}{12} = 2.304 \text{ m}^4$$

For both sides (port + starboard):
$$I_{sides} = 2 \times 2.304 = 4.608 \text{ m}^4$$

For **deck plate** (horizontal, treated as equivalent flange at z = 6 m above baseline):
- A_d = 18 × 0.018 = 0.324 m²
- $\bar{z}$ from NA = 0 (deck plate at z = 12 → distance from NA = 6 m)
- $A_d \bar{z}^2 = 0.324 \times 6^2 = 0.324 \times 36 = 11.664$ m⁴
- Self moment negligible: $\dfrac{18 \times 0.018^3}{12} \approx 0.0000087$ m⁴

Similarly for **bottom plate** (at z = 0 → distance from NA = 6 m):
- A_b = 18 × 0.016 = 0.288 m²
- $A_b \bar{z}^2 = 0.288 \times 36 = 10.368$ m⁴

**Step 4:** Total I_NA:
$$
I_{NA} = I_{sides} + A_d \bar{z}_d^2 + A_b \bar{z}_b^2
$$
$$
I_{NA} = 4.608 + 11.664 + 10.368 = 26.640 \text{ m}^4
$$

**Step 5:** Section modulus:
$$
Z = \frac{I_{NA}}{z_{max}} = \frac{26.640}{6} = 4.440 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 6:** Check:
$$
Z_{actual} = 4.440 \text{ m}^3 > Z_{required} = 2.5 \text{ m}^3 \quad \checkmark \text{ ADEQUATE}
$$

(2 marks: I_sides, 2 marks: I_flanges, 1 mark: I_total, 1 mark: Z, 1 mark: verdict.)

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Midship Section / Shell Expansion Problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing **mm** and **m** within the same equation. ALWAYS convert to SI base units (m, N, Pa) or all to mm-N-MPa before computing. (Loss: 1–2 marks)
>
> 2. **Forgetting to Use Effective Breadth:** In modern rules (CSR), only a fraction of plate width (effective breadth b_eff = min(s, L_stiff/2)) contributes to section modulus. Ignoring this gives **overestimated Z**, an unsafe answer.
>
> 3. **Treating NA at Geometric Center:** For an asymmetric section (more material on deck than bottom, common in ships), the NA is NOT at D/2. Always compute from $\sum A_i z_i = 0$.
>
> 4. **Confusing Hogging and Sagging:** In **hogging**, the deck is in **tension** (lower stress for steel, good), bottom in **compression** (buckling risk). In **sagging**, the opposite. The KTU paper often tests which one governs for a given vessel.
>
> 5. **Shell Expansion Drawing Scale:** Students forget to mention scale (1:50, 1:100). Loss: 0.5–1 mark.
>
> 6. **Plate Numbering Confusion:** Plates are numbered from **keel → sheer**, **stern → bow**. Wrong direction = 1 mark lost.
>
> 7. **Not Mentioning Welding Edge Preparation:** Shell expansion plates need **beveled edges** (e.g., double-V groove for thick plates). Skipping this is a common professional mistake.
>
> 8. **Using M_w without M_s:** Total design moment = M_s + M_w, not just M_w. This is a frequent trap.

---

## 5.4 ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LAST-MINUTE REVISION BULLETS — KTU Module 3, PCNST502**
>
> - 🔹 **Ship structural design** = determination of scantlings to safely resist all in-service loads over design life.
> - 🔹 **Loads** are categorized as **Global** (hull girder M, F, T) and **Local** (hydrostatic, slamming, cargo, tank, deck).
> - 🔹 **Rule-based design** uses Classification Society (IRS, LR, DNV, ABS) empirical formulas.
> - 🔹 **Common Structural Rules (CSR-B, CSR-T)** introduced partial safety factor (limit state) design for tankers and bulk carriers.
> - 🔹 **Midship section** = 0.4L from AP, the most loaded region; design starts here.
> - 🔹 **Bending stress formula:** $\sigma = M/Z$ — memorize and use correctly.
> - 🔹 **Section modulus** = $Z = I/z_{max}$, where I = $\sum A_i z_i^2$ (parallel axis theorem).
> - 🔹 **Neutral axis location** comes from $\sum A_i z_i = 0$, NOT assumed at mid-depth for asymmetric sections.
> - 🔹 **Permissible stress** = $\sigma_y / SF$. For mild steel: SF = 1.43–1.67.
> - 🔹 **Hogging vs Sagging** — know which is critical for tankers (sagging) vs bulk carriers (hogging).
> - 🔹 **Higher Tensile Steel (HTS)** Grade AH32 (σ_y = 315 MPa) used where weight saving or higher strength needed.
> - 🔹 **Shear strength** check uses Schur's formula: $\tau = FQ/(It)$.
> - 🔹 **Plate thickness** from rule: $t = (s \sqrt{p})/(k \sqrt{\sigma_y})$, where s = spacing, p = pressure.
> - 🔹 **Frame spacing** for bottom shell typically 600–750 mm for longitudinal framing, 800–1000 mm for transverse framing.
> - 🔹 **Shell expansion drawing** = 2D flattening of 3D hull surface for fabrication.
> - 🔹 **Development methods:** Parallel line (developable), Triangulation (non-developable), Computer-aided (NAPA, AVEVA, FORAN).
> - 🔹 **Plate numbering** = from keel to sheer, stern to bow. Use plate schedule tables.
> - 🔹 **Shell expansion drawings** must include plate boundaries, frame lines, waterlines, plate numbers, thickness, material grade, weld prep, scale, title block.
> - 🔹 **Effective breadth rule** reduces flange contribution to prevent overestimation of section modulus.
> - 🔹 **Buckling** is critical for slender plates in compression — use $\sigma_{cr} = (k \pi^2 E)/(12(1-\nu^2)) (t/s)^2$.
> - 🔹 **Corrosion addition** = +1 to +3 mm to rule thickness depending on service.
> - 🔹 **Class symbols** (e.g., ✠100A1) on midship section drawing indicate rule compliance.
> - 🔹 **Midship section drawing** must show all effective longitudinals, transverse webs, openings with compensation, brackets, and slot welds.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 3-D Modelling of Ship Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST502 - SHIP STRUCTURES I
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** 3-D Modelling of Ship Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**3-D Modelling of Ship Structures** is the computer-aided process of mathematically and geometrically representing the complete load-bearing skeleton of a ship — including the hull girder, decks, bulkheads, stiffeners, girders, and foundations — as a discretized **finite element mesh** in three-dimensional space, in order to predict its strength, deformation, vibration, and failure characteristics under operational and environmental loads.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for PCNST502 (Ship Structures I), this topic forms the bridge between classical structural analysis (hand-calculation of beam-girder strength, plate bending, and section modulus) and the **modern Class-society-approved Computer-Aided Engineering (CAE)** workflow used in shipyards and design offices worldwide.

The three pillars of ship-structure 3D modelling are:
1. **Geometric idealization** (lines-plan → fair surface → plate/stiffener assembly)
2. **Discretization** (continuous structure → finite elements)
3. **Numerical solution** (stiffness assembly → load application → solver → post-processing)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly states that students must be able to *“model a representative ship structural component (e.g., a midship section, a transverse ring frame, or a hatch corner) in 3D, apply appropriate boundary conditions, and interpret the resulting stress contours and deflection plots.”*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are building a **giant cardboard model of a ship** for an architectural exhibition. You do not use a single solid piece of cardboard. Instead, you cut the hull into **hundreds of small triangular and rectangular cardboard tiles**, glue them together along their edges, and then support the whole assembly at specific points (the keel and the supports on the display table).

- Each **tile** is a *finite element*.
- The **glue line stiffness** is the *element stiffness matrix*.
- The way the tiles transmit forces to the supports is the *global stiffness equation*.
- The shape the ship takes under a "wind load" you simulate with your hand is the *deflected shape* from the solver.

A 3D ship structural model is **exactly this** — except the cardboard is steel, the glue is mathematics, and the wind is the **wave-induced hydrodynamic pressure** on the hull plating. The engineer builds a digital twin of the ship and shakes it, bends it, and twists it on the computer *before* a single plate is cut in the yard.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics (Bolded)

- **Reference design wave height (H):** typically **3.0 m to 12.0 m** for merchant ships
- **Young's Modulus of shipbuilding steel (E):** **2.05 × 10⁵ N/mm² (≈ 206 GPa)**
- **Poisson's ratio (ν):** **0.30** for mild steel
- **Yield stress (σ_y) of grade A/AH steel:** **235 N/mm²**
- **Yield stress of grade DH/EH steel:** **315 – 390 N/mm²**
- **Mesh density in way of stress concentration:** element size **≤ 50 mm**
- **Coarse mesh in midship field:** element size **≈ 200–400 mm**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 3D and not 2D?** A ship is essentially a thin-walled box girder subjected to **sagging**, **hogging**, **torsion**, and **lateral bending** simultaneously. Classical 2D beam theory (e.g., simple beam bending: σ = M·y/I) misses the **local plate bending**, **shear lag**, and **stress concentration at openings** (manholes, hatch corners, bracket toes). Hence 3D FEM is mandatory for any modern rule-based strength check.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 3D Modelling Pipeline — Step-by-Step Logic

The engineering workflow is divided into **seven sequential stages**. Each stage has a clear "why" and "how":

| Stage | What is done? | Why is it done? | How is it done? |
|-------|---------------|------------------|------------------|
| **1. Lines Plan & Fairing** | Hull form defined by offsets | Get smooth, fair surface for plating development | Spline interpolation of station offsets |
| **2. Structural Idealization** | Plates → 2D shell elements; stiffeners → 1D beam elements | Reduce DOFs without losing accuracy | Mid-surface offset for plates; eccentric beam offset for stiffeners |
| **3. Meshing (Discretization)** | Continuous domain → finite elements | Convert PDE to algebraic system | Quadrilateral (Q4) or triangular (CST/LST) elements for plates; 2-node beam for stiffeners |
| **4. Material & Section Properties** | Assign E, ν, σ_y, t, I, A | Quantify stiffness and strength | Steel tables; section modulus from scantlings |
| **5. Boundary Conditions** | Fix selected nodes (u = v = w = 0) | Prevent rigid-body motion | Symmetry planes, supports, fixed ends |
| **6. Load Application** | Hydrostatic + wave + cargo + inertia | Simulate real in-service loads | Pressure on wetted surface; point/moment loads at bulkheads |
| **7. Solution & Post-Processing** | Solve [K]{U} = {F} | Find nodal displacements → stresses | Direct solver (Gauss elimination) or iterative (PCG) |

## 2.2 Element Library Used in Ship Modelling

| Element Type | DOFs per node | Used for | KTU relevance |
|--------------|---------------|----------|----------------|
| **Membrane (CST)** | 2 in-plane (u, v) | Plating in-plane action | Module 3 prep |
| **Plate-bending (DKT / Mindlin)** | 3 (w, θ_x, θ_y) | Plating out-of-plane bending | Module 4 core |
| **Shell (combined)** | 5 or 6 | Hull plating, bulkhead plating | Module 4 core |
| **Beam (2-node Euler-Bernoulli)** | 3 per node | Stiffeners, frames, girders | Module 1–2 recall |
| **Solid (8-node hex / 4-node tet)** | 3 per node | Bracket toes, hatch corners | Stress concentration |

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept Reinforcement:** A *midship 3D model* of a Panamax bulk carrier typically contains **300,000 to 1,500,000 elements** and **150,000 to 800,000 nodes**. Even a "small" FE model in ship design is far larger than what a hand-calculation could ever handle — which is precisely why this topic exists in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $[K]_{global} \cdot \{U\} = \{F\}$ | Global equilibrium equation (linear static) | N, mm |
| 2 | $[K]^e = \int_{V_e} [B]^T [D] [B] \, dV$ | Element stiffness integral | N/mm |
| 3 | $\sigma = [D][B]\{u\}^e$ | Stress from nodal displacement | N/mm² |
| 4 | $E_{eff} = \dfrac{E}{1-\nu^2}$ | Plate-modulus (plane strain) | N/mm² |
| 5 | $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 - \sigma_x \sigma_y + 3\tau_{xy}^2}$ | Von Mises equivalent stress | N/mm² |
| 6 | $D = \dfrac{E\,t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ | Flexural rigidity of plate (per unit width) | N·mm |
| 7 | $f_{mn} = \dfrac{\pi}{2}\left[ \left(\dfrac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{n}{b}\right)^2 \right]\sqrt{\dfrac{D}{\rho\,t}}$ | Natural frequency of simply-supported plate | Hz |
| 8 | $M_p = \sigma_y \cdot Z_p$ | Plastic moment of beam-girder | N·mm |
| 9 | $\sigma_{bending} = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Classical beam bending stress (recall) | N/mm² |
| 10 | $\sigma_{shear\,lag\,factor} = \dfrac{\sigma_{max}}{\sigma_{simple}}$ | Shear-lag amplification (DNV criterion) | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constraint reminder:** In all table cells above, the symbol *pipe* has been deliberately written as `\vert` so as to NOT break the markdown table syntax. Students should copy these into their revision notes exactly as shown.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

3D ship structural modelling is the **backbone of rule-based classification**. Every major class society (DNV, LR, ABS, BV, CCS) now demands finite-element analysis evidence for:

- **Hatch corner fatigue** assessment (common cause of bulk-carrier failures)
- **Slamming-induced bottom plate** yielding
- **Twisting moment** in very large container ships
- **Vibration of superstructure** in cruise liners and naval combatants

A naval architect without command over 3D modelling today is, in practice, unemployable in any design office.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** The following derivations form the **analytical heart of Module 4**. In a 14-mark KTU question, students are routinely expected to derive the **stiffness matrix of a CST (Constant Strain Triangle)** element and the **stiffness matrix of a 2-node beam element** along with the **transformation to 3D space**.

---

## 3.1 Derivation — Stiffness Matrix of a 2-Node Beam Element (Euler-Bernoulli)

This is the building block for every stiffener, frame, and girder in a 3D ship model.

### Step 1: Degrees of Freedom
For a planar beam element of length L, with two nodes (1 and 2), each node has **2 DOFs** — transverse displacement (w) and rotation (θ). Total = 4 DOFs:

$$
\{u\}^e = \begin{Bmatrix} w_1 & \theta_1 & w_2 & \theta_2 \end{Bmatrix}^T
$$

### Step 2: Assumed Displacement Field (Hermite Shape Functions)
A fourth-order Hermite polynomial is assumed to satisfy the Euler-Bernoulli kinematic hypothesis (plane sections remain plane, deflections small).

$$
w(x) = N_1 w_1 + N_2 \theta_1 + N_3 w_2 + N_4 \theta_2
$$

with the cubic shape functions:

$$
N_1 = 1 - 3\left(\dfrac{x}{L}\right)^2 + 2\left(\dfrac{x}{L}\right)^3
$$

$$
N_2 = x - \dfrac{2x^2}{L} + \dfrac{x^3}{L^2}
$$

$$
N_3 = 3\left(\dfrac{x}{L}\right)^2 - 2\left(\dfrac{x}{L}\right)^3
$$

$$
N_4 = -\dfrac{x^2}{L} + \dfrac{x^3}{L^2}
$$

### Step 3: Strain–Displacement Matrix [B]
Curvature is the second derivative of deflection:

$$
\kappa(x) = \dfrac{d^2 w}{dx^2} = \dfrac{d^2 N_i}{dx^2} u_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad [B] = \dfrac{d^2 [N]}{dx^2}
$$

Differentiating twice and writing in compact form:

$$
[B] = \dfrac{1}{L^3}\begin{bmatrix} -6+12\xi & L(4-6\xi) & 6-12\xi & L(2-6\xi) \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $\xi = x/L$.

### Step 4: Element Stiffness Integral

$$
[K]^e = \int_0^L [B]^T \, E I \, [B] \, dx
$$

Substituting [B] and integrating each term over the length 0 → L:

$$
[K]^e = \dfrac{EI}{L^3} \begin{bmatrix}
12 & 6L & -12 & 6L \\
6L & 4L^2 & -6L & 2L^2 \\
-12 & -6L & 12 & -6L \\
6L & 2L^2 & -6L & 4L^2
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 5: 3D Transformation
When the beam is oriented along an arbitrary direction in 3D space, defined by direction cosines (l, m, n), the 6-DOF-per-node (3 translations + 3 rotations) global stiffness is obtained via a 12×12 transformation matrix [T]:

$$
[K]_{global}^e = [T]^T [K]^e [T]
$$

where [T] is built from the **direction-cosine matrix** of the beam axis.

> **Result:** This $[K]^e$ is what gets assembled for every stiffener in the 3D ship model. The vector $\{F\}$ contains distributed pressure loads (converted to equivalent nodal forces) and the inertia loads in a dynamic analysis.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Stiffness Matrix of a CST (Constant Strain Triangle) Element

The CST is the workhorse for in-plane analysis of ship plating and panels.

### Step 1: Element Geometry
A triangular element with three nodes (i, j, m), each with 2 in-plane DOFs: u, v. Area:

$$
A = \dfrac{1}{2}\begin{vmatrix} 1 & x_i & y_i \\ 1 & x_j & y_j \\ 1 & x_m & y_m \end{vmatrix}
$$

### Step 2: Linear Shape Functions
Because the strain is constant (hence "Constant Strain"), displacements vary linearly:

$$
u(x,y) = N_i u_i + N_j u_j + N_m u_m
$$

$$
v(x,y) = N_i v_i + N_j v_j + N_m v_m
$$

with:

$$
N_i = \dfrac{1}{2A}(a_i + b_i x + c_i y)
$$

$$
a_i = x_j y_m - x_m y_j, \quad b_i = y_j - y_m, \quad c_i = x_m - x_j
$$

(cyclically for j, m).

### Step 3: Strain–Displacement Matrix [B]
Strain vector $\{\varepsilon\} = \{\varepsilon_x,\ \varepsilon_y,\ \gamma_{xy}\}^T$:

$$
[B] = \dfrac{1}{2A}\begin{bmatrix}
b_i & 0 & b_j & 0 & b_m & 0 \\
0 & c_i & 0 & c_j & 0 & c_m \\
c_i & b_i & c_j & b_j & c_m & b_m
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4: Material Matrix [D] (Plane Stress, ship plating)
For a plate of thickness t, plane stress assumption:

$$
[D] = \dfrac{E}{1-\nu^2}\begin{bmatrix}
1 & \nu & 0 \\
\nu & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & \dfrac{1-\nu}{2}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 5: Element Stiffness
Since [B] is constant over the triangle:

$$
[K]^e = t \cdot A \cdot [B]^T [D] [B]
$$

The 6×6 matrix is symmetric, with **constant strain** — therefore **constant stress** per element.

> **Result:** CST is the simplest plate element, but is overly stiff in bending. For ship-plate bending, the DKT (Discrete Kirchhoff Triangle) or Mindlin shell element is preferred.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Natural Frequency of a Simply-Supported Rectangular Plate

This is the classic **Karman plate vibration** result, frequently asked in 14-mark KTU problems.

### Step 1: Governing PDE
For a thin plate of flexural rigidity D, mass per unit area $\bar{m} = \rho t$:

$$
D \nabla^4 w + \bar{m}\,\ddot{w} = 0
$$

### Step 2: Navier's Solution
For simply-supported edges, the assumed mode is:

$$
w(x,y,t) = W_{mn} \sin\!\left(\dfrac{m\pi x}{a}\right) \sin\!\left(\dfrac{n\pi y}{b}\right) e^{i\omega t}
$$

### Step 3: Substitute into the PDE

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla^4 w &= \pi^4 \left[\left(\dfrac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{n}{b}\right)^2\right]^2 w \\
\Rightarrow\quad & -D\,\pi^4 \left[\left(\dfrac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{n}{b}\right)^2\right]^2 W_{mn} - \bar{m}\,\omega^2 W_{mn} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Solve for $\omega$
Setting the coefficient of $W_{mn}$ to zero:

$$
\omega_{mn}^2 = \dfrac{D\,\pi^4}{\bar{m}}\left[\left(\dfrac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{n}{b}\right)^2\right]^2
$$

### Step 5: Frequency in Hz

$$
f_{mn} = \dfrac{\omega_{mn}}{2\pi} = \dfrac{\pi}{2}\left[\left(\dfrac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{n}{b}\right)^2\right]\sqrt{\dfrac{D}{\rho\,t}}
$$

with $D = \dfrac{E\,t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$.

### Verification
For a 1 m × 1 m × 10 mm steel plate (m = n = 1):

$$
D = \dfrac{2.05 \times 10^5 \times 10^3}{12(1-0.09)} \approx 1.88 \times 10^7\ \text{N·mm}
$$

$$
f_{11} = \dfrac{\pi}{2}\left(1+1\right)\sqrt{\dfrac{1.88 \times 10^7}{7.85 \times 10^{-9} \times 7.85 \times 10^3 \times 10}} \approx 31\ \text{Hz}
$$

> **Engineering meaning:** This 31 Hz natural frequency of the unsupported plating sets the **lower bound** for the natural frequency of any stiffened panel in the 3D model. If engine-order excitation or propeller-induced pressure fluctuations hit this frequency, resonance will occur and the plating will fail by fatigue in a matter of months.

```mermaid
    K3 --> L
```


## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Classification Society Strength Check Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    R2 --> R3{DNV or LR or ABS Check}
    R3 --> R31[Yield Check sigma less than sigma_y over k]
    R3 --> R32[Buckling Check for Plates and Stiffeners]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define 3-D modelling of ship structures. List the major steps involved in the pre-processing stage.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

3-D modelling of ship structures is the process of creating a discretized finite-element representation of the ship's load-bearing components in three-dimensional space, so that its strength, deformation, and dynamic behaviour can be analysed numerically.

The major pre-processing steps are:

1. **Lines plan & fairing** — generating a smooth hull surface from offset data.
2. **Structural idealization** — converting plating to shell elements, stiffeners to beam elements.
3. **Mesh generation** — discretizing the continuous domain into finite elements.
4. **Assignment of material and section properties** — E, ν, t, I, A, σ_y.
5. **Application of boundary conditions** — symmetry, fixed supports, neutral-axis constraints.

> Each step carries 0.5 mark, definition carries 0.5 mark — total **3 marks**.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
**What is a Constant Strain Triangle (CST) element? Why is it called "constant strain"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A CST is a 2-D triangular finite element with **three nodes**, each having **two in-plane degrees of freedom (u, v)**. It is the simplest plane-stress / plane-strain element used in modelling the in-plane action of ship plating.

It is called "constant strain" because the **shape functions are linear** in x and y, so the **derivatives** (which give the strain) are **constants** over the entire triangular area. Consequently, the stress computed within the element is also constant (no variation across the element), making it a low-order but computationally efficient element.

> First paragraph = 1.5 marks, explanation of "constant strain" = 1.5 marks — total **3 marks**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the complete 3-D modelling workflow of a typical **midship section of a bulk carrier**, starting from the lines plan up to post-processing. Mention the typical element types, mesh sizes, and boundary conditions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A simply-supported rectangular steel plate in a ship's side shell has dimensions a = 1500 mm, b = 1000 mm, thickness t = 12 mm. The plate is of grade A steel. Calculate the **fundamental natural frequency** of the plate. Use E = 2.05 × 10⁵ N/mm², ν = 0.30, ρ = 7.85 × 10⁻⁹ tonne/mm³. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) 3-D Modelling Workflow (7 marks):**

| Step | Description | Marks |
|------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | **Lines plan & fairing** — Generate hull surface from station offsets. | 1 |
| 2 | **Structural idealization** — Plating as 4-node shell elements; longitudinals and transverse frames as 2-node beam elements offset from plate mid-surface. | 1.5 |
| 3 | **Mesh generation** — Typical mesh size: 200 × 200 mm in field areas; refined to 50 × 50 mm in way of hatch corners and openings. | 1 |
| 4 | **Material properties** — E = 2.05 × 10⁵ N/mm², ν = 0.30, σ_y = 235 N/mm² (Grade A). | 0.5 |
| 5 | **Boundary conditions** — Symmetry planes at forward and aft ends of the modelled length; fixed translations at the neutral axis at the ends to prevent rigid-body motion. | 1.5 |
| 6 | **Loading** — Static sea pressure (ρgh), wave-induced bending moment (sagging & hogging), cargo pressure on inner bottom. | 1 |
| 7 | **Post-processing** — Stress contour plots (von Mises), deflection plots, mode shapes for vibration. | 0.5 |

**Total = 7 marks**

**(b) Natural Frequency Calculation (7 marks):**

Given: a = 1500 mm, b = 1000 mm, t = 12 mm, E = 2.05 × 10⁵ N/mm², ν = 0.30, ρ = 7.85 × 10⁻⁹ t/mm³.

**Step 1: Flexural rigidity of the plate:**

$$
D = \dfrac{E t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)} = \dfrac{2.05 \times 10^5 \times 12^3}{12(1 - 0.30^2)}
$$

$$
D = \dfrac{2.05 \times 10^5 \times 1728}{12 \times 0.91} = \dfrac{3.5424 \times 10^8}{10.92} \approx 3.244 \times 10^7\ \text{N·mm}
$$

**Step 2: Mass per unit area:**

$$
\bar{m} = \rho \, t = 7.85 \times 10^{-9} \times 12 = 9.42 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{t/mm}^2
$$

**Step 3: Fundamental mode (m = 1, n = 1):**

$$
f_{11} = \dfrac{\pi}{2}\left[\left(\dfrac{1}{1500}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{1}{1000}\right)^2\right]\sqrt{\dfrac{D}{\bar{m}}}
$$

$$
\left(\dfrac{1}{1500}\right)^2 = 4.44 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{mm}^{-2}
$$

$$
\left(\dfrac{1}{1000}\right)^2 = 1.00 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{mm}^{-2}
$$

$$
\text{Sum} = 1.444 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{mm}^{-2}
$$

$$
\sqrt{\dfrac{D}{\bar{m}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3.244 \times 10^7}{9.42 \times 10^{-8}}} = \sqrt{3.443 \times 10^{14}} = 1.856 \times 10^7\ \text{mm/s}
$$

**Step 4: Final frequency:**

$$
f_{11} = \dfrac{\pi}{2} \times 1.444 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.856 \times 10^7
$$

$$
f_{11} = 1.5708 \times 1.444 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.856 \times 10^7 \approx 42.1\ \text{Hz}
$$

**Final Answer:** $f_{11} \approx 42.1\ \text{Hz}$ — **1 mark**

> **Distribution:** Step 1 = 2 marks, Step 2 = 1 mark, Step 3 = 2 marks, Step 4 = 1 mark, Final answer = 1 mark.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the **stiffness matrix of a 2-node Euler-Bernoulli beam element** with cubic Hermite shape functions, showing the displacement field, strain–displacement matrix, and the final 4 × 4 stiffness matrix. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the importance of **mesh quality, boundary conditions, and element selection** in 3D ship structural modelling. State the consequences of poor mesh and incorrect boundary conditions with two examples. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Stiffness Matrix Derivation (7 marks):**

The complete derivation has been covered in **Section 3.1** above. The marks breakdown is:

| Sub-step | Marks |
|----------|-------|
| DOF definition and assumed displacement field with cubic shape functions | 2 |
| Strain–displacement matrix [B] (second derivative of shape functions) | 2 |
| Element stiffness integral evaluation | 2 |
| Final 4 × 4 symmetric matrix in EI/L³ form | 1 |

Final expression to be reproduced:

$$
[K]^e = \dfrac{EI}{L^3} \begin{bmatrix}
12 & 6L & -12 & 6L \\
6L & 4L^2 & -6L & 2L^2 \\
-12 & -6L & 12 & -6L \\
6L & 2L^2 & -6L & 4L^2
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**(b) Importance of Mesh Quality, BCs, and Element Selection (7 marks):**

**Mesh quality (2.5 marks):**
- Aspect ratio of elements should be ≤ 3:1 for ship plating meshes.
- Skewness (deviation of angles from 90° in quads) should be ≤ 30°.
- Poor mesh → inaccurate stress, exaggerated deflections, solver divergence.
- *Example:* Using a coarse 400 mm mesh in the hatch corner of a bulk carrier will **underestimate the peak stress** by 40–60%, missing a critical fatigue location.

**Boundary conditions (2.5 marks):**
- Incorrect BCs → wrong deformation pattern; can introduce artificial stress concentrations.
- *Example:* Forgetting to apply symmetry at the ends of a half-hull model allows **rigid-body motion** in the longitudinal direction, producing spurious stresses and a non-converging solver.

**Element selection (2 marks):**
- CST is acceptable for in-plane action but overstiff for plate bending.
- *Example:* Using CST for a stiffened side panel in bending will predict deflections that are 30–50% lower than the actual measured values, leading to a non-conservative design.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **In derivation questions:** Skipping the shape-function differentiation and directly writing the [B] matrix — **lose 1 mark**.
> 2. **In numerical problems:** Forgetting to convert the unit of density (tonne/mm³ ↔ kg/m³) consistently. This single mistake can throw the final answer off by **2–3 orders of magnitude** — **lose 2 marks**.
> 3. **In descriptive answers:** Writing "we use FEM" without specifying *element type*, *mesh size*, and *boundary condition* — **lose 1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Common blunder:** Computing von Mises stress without including the **out-of-plane shear** from the plate-bending element — **lose 1 mark**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid- revision checklist (read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- ✅ 3-D modelling = **Geometric idealization + Discretization + Numerical solution**.
- ✅ Plates → **Shell elements (4-node Mindlin or DKT)**; Stiffeners → **Beam elements (2-node, eccentric offset)**.
- ✅ The governing equation is always **[K]{U} = {F}** — assemble K, apply F, solve for U, back-calculate σ.
- ✅ For a CST element, **strain is constant** over the triangle (linear shape functions).
- ✅ The **beam stiffness matrix** uses **cubic Hermite shape functions** and gives the familiar 4 × 4 matrix proportional to **EI/L³**.
- ✅ Fundamental plate natural frequency: $f_{11} = \dfrac{\pi}{2}\left[\left(\dfrac{1}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{1}{b}\right)^2\right]\sqrt{\dfrac{D}{\rho t}}$.
- ✅ **Flexural rigidity:** $D = \dfrac{E t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ — note the plane-strain factor $(1-\nu^2)$.
- ✅ Von Mises stress: $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 - \sigma_x\sigma_y + 3\tau_{xy}^2}$.
- ✅ **Steel constants to memorize:** E = 2.05 × 10⁵ N/mm², ν = 0.30, ρ = 7.85 t/m³.
- ✅ **Mesh rule of thumb:** 50 mm at stress hotspots, 200–400 mm in field areas.
- ✅ **Boundary conditions** must always prevent **rigid-body motion** (minimum 6 DOFs constrained in 3D).
- ✅ **Class-society rule checks** (yield, buckling, fatigue) are the *acceptance criteria* of any 3D ship model.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — 3-D Modelling of Ship Structures.**
